name: AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based
description: Master every concept tested on the AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based exam — from kinematics to fluid dynamics. Covers all 8 CED units aligned to the College Board Course and Exam Description (Fall 2024). Each unit includes equation analysis, conceptual understanding, and common misconception busters.

facts.correctAnswer: Scalar
acceptableAlternatives: scalar quantity
facts.quizQuestion: What type of physical quantity has magnitude only and no direction?
facts.explanation: Scalars are fully described by a number and unit alone — no direction needed. Examples: distance (5 m), speed (30 m/s), mass (2 kg), temperature (300 K), and time (10 s). Confusing scalars with vectors is a common error on the AP exam.
facts.visualDescription: A speedometer showing only a magnitude number with no arrow

facts.correctAnswer: Vector
acceptableAlternatives: vector quantity
facts.quizQuestion: What type of physical quantity requires both magnitude AND direction to fully describe it?
facts.explanation: Vectors need both a magnitude and a direction. Examples: displacement (5 m north), velocity (30 m/s east), acceleration (9.8 m/s² downward), and force (10 N at 30°). Vectors are often drawn as arrows, where length represents magnitude and orientation shows direction.
facts.visualDescription: An arrow labeled with both a length (magnitude) and an angle showing direction

facts.correctAnswer: Distance
acceptableAlternatives: total path length
facts.quizQuestion: Which quantity measures total path length traveled and is a scalar (not a vector)?
facts.explanation: Distance measures the total length of the path traveled, regardless of direction. If you walk 3 m east then 4 m west, your distance is 7 m. It is always non-negative and has no directional component, making it a scalar.
facts.visualDescription: A curvy path with total length labeled as distance

facts.correctAnswer: Displacement
acceptableAlternatives: change in position
Δx
facts.quizQuestion: What vector quantity equals the change in position from start to finish, regardless of path taken?
facts.explanation: Displacement is defined as Δx = x_final − x_initial. It measures the straight-line change in position and includes direction. Walking 3 m east then 4 m west gives a displacement of 1 m west — not 7 m. Displacement can be zero even when distance is large (e.g., completing a lap on a track).
facts.visualDescription: A straight arrow from start point to end point overlaid on a curved path

facts.correctAnswer: Δx = x − x₀
acceptableAlternatives: x_f minus x_i
final position minus initial position
facts.quizQuestion: What equation defines displacement (Δx) in one dimension?
facts.explanation: Displacement Δx equals the final position x minus the initial position x₀. This simple subtraction gives you a signed result: positive values indicate motion in the positive direction, negative values indicate motion in the negative direction. It's the foundation of all kinematic equations.
facts.visualDescription: A number line showing x₀ and x with an arrow labeled Δx between them

facts.correctAnswer: Average velocity
acceptableAlternatives: v_avg
displacement divided by time
facts.quizQuestion: What vector quantity equals displacement divided by the elapsed time (Δx/Δt)?
facts.explanation: Average velocity = Δx/Δt. Because displacement is a vector, average velocity is also a vector — it has both magnitude and direction. It is NOT the same as average speed (which uses distance, a scalar). A car that returns to its starting point has zero average velocity for the trip, even though its average speed is non-zero.
facts.visualDescription: Equation v_avg = Δx/Δt on a chalkboard with a direction arrow

facts.correctAnswer: v_avg = Δx / Δt
acceptableAlternatives: displacement over time interval
Δx/Δt
facts.quizQuestion: What is the defining equation for average velocity?
facts.explanation: v_avg = Δx/Δt uses displacement (not distance) in the numerator. The SI unit is m/s. For an object moving at constant velocity, average velocity equals instantaneous velocity at every moment. For non-constant motion, v_avg gives only the overall rate of displacement change.
facts.visualDescription: Fraction showing Δx over Δt with vector arrows indicating direction

facts.correctAnswer: a_avg = Δv / Δt
acceptableAlternatives: change in velocity over time
Δv/Δt
facts.quizQuestion: What equation defines average acceleration?
facts.explanation: Average acceleration = Δv/Δt, where Δv = v_final − v_initial. Acceleration is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. An object can accelerate even while slowing down if acceleration opposes the direction of motion (deceleration). SI unit: m/s².
facts.visualDescription: Equation a = Δv/Δt with velocity vectors at two different times

facts.correctAnswer: v = v₀ + at
acceptableAlternatives: v final equals v initial plus a times t
facts.quizQuestion: Which kinematic equation relates final velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and time — with no displacement term?
facts.explanation: v = v₀ + at applies when acceleration is constant. It is derived directly from the definition of acceleration: a = Δv/Δt → v − v₀ = at → v = v₀ + at. Use this when displacement is unknown or not needed. It is valid only for uniform (constant) acceleration.
facts.visualDescription: Velocity-time graph with a straight line rising from v₀ to v over time t

facts.correctAnswer: x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at²
acceptableAlternatives: displacement equals v-naught t plus one-half a t squared
facts.quizQuestion: Which kinematic equation gives position as a function of time for constant acceleration (includes a ½at² term)?
facts.explanation: x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at² describes position at any time t under constant acceleration. The ½at² term accounts for the changing velocity during the time interval. For free fall starting from rest (v₀ = 0), this reduces to x = x₀ + ½gt².
facts.visualDescription: Parabolic trajectory of an object showing position growing quadratically with time

facts.correctAnswer: v² = v₀² + 2a(x − x₀)
acceptableAlternatives: v squared equals v-naught squared plus 2a delta x
facts.quizQuestion: Which kinematic equation relates velocity, acceleration, and displacement — with NO time variable?
facts.explanation: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx is the time-independent kinematic equation. Use it when time is unknown and you need to find final velocity or displacement. For free fall: v² = v₀² + 2g(−h) when taking downward as negative. It is derived by eliminating t from the other two kinematic equations.
facts.visualDescription: A ball dropped from height h with arrows showing v₀=0, v, and Δx

acceptableAlternatives: approximately 10 m/s² downward
9.8 m/s²
g ≈ 10 m/s²
facts.quizQuestion: What is the approximate magnitude of gravitational acceleration near Earth's surface used in AP Physics 1?
facts.explanation: AP Physics 1 uses g ≈ 10 m/s² for calculations (the more precise value is 9.8 m/s²). This acceleration is directed downward toward Earth's center. All freely falling objects near Earth's surface — regardless of mass — experience this same downward acceleration (neglecting air resistance).
facts.visualDescription: A falling ball with a downward arrow labeled g = 10 m/s²

facts.correctAnswer: Instantaneous velocity
acceptableAlternatives: velocity at that instant
slope of x-t graph equals velocity
facts.quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the slope of a position-time (x-t) graph represent?
facts.explanation: Slope = rise/run = Δx/Δt = velocity. On an x-t graph, a steep positive slope means fast motion in the positive direction; a negative slope means motion in the negative direction; a horizontal line means the object is at rest. A curved x-t graph has a slope (velocity) that changes over time.
facts.visualDescription: Position-time graph with tangent line drawn at a point, labeled 'slope = velocity'

facts.correctAnswer: Instantaneous acceleration
acceptableAlternatives: acceleration
slope of v-t graph equals acceleration
facts.quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the slope of a velocity-time (v-t) graph represent?
facts.explanation: Slope = Δv/Δt = acceleration. A positive slope on a v-t graph means the object is speeding up in the positive direction; a negative slope could mean slowing down (if v is positive) or speeding up in the negative direction. A horizontal v-t line means zero acceleration (constant velocity).
facts.visualDescription: Velocity-time graph with slope triangle labeled Δv over Δt = acceleration

facts.correctAnswer: Displacement
acceptableAlternatives: change in position
Δx
facts.quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the area under a velocity-time (v-t) graph represent?
facts.explanation: Area under v-t graph = ∫v dt = displacement. For a constant velocity, the area is a rectangle (v × t = displacement). For constant acceleration, the area is a trapezoid or triangle. Crucially, area below the time axis counts as negative displacement (motion in the negative direction).
facts.visualDescription: Shaded area under a v-t graph labeled 'area = displacement'

facts.correctAnswer: Change in velocity (Δv)
acceptableAlternatives: velocity change
Δv
facts.quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the area under an acceleration-time (a-t) graph represent?
facts.explanation: Area under a-t graph = ∫a dt = Δv (change in velocity). This is the graphical interpretation of a_avg = Δv/Δt rearranged as Δv = a × Δt. It is NOT displacement; that would be the area under the v-t graph.
facts.visualDescription: Shaded area under an a-t graph with label 'area = Δv'

facts.correctAnswer: Inertial reference frame
acceptableAlternatives: inertial frame
facts.quizQuestion: What type of reference frame moves at constant velocity (zero acceleration), so Newton's laws hold without modification?
facts.explanation: An inertial reference frame is one that is not accelerating. In such frames, an object with no net force remains at constant velocity (Newton's First Law works directly). The laws of physics take the same form in all inertial frames — a key principle underlying classical mechanics. A rotating frame is non-inertial.
facts.visualDescription: Two trains moving at constant velocity past each other, labeled as inertial frames

facts.correctAnswer: Relative velocity
acceptableAlternatives: velocity relative to a reference frame
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the velocity of an object as measured from a particular reference frame, which can differ between observers?
facts.explanation: Relative velocity depends on the observer's frame. A passenger on a train moving at 60 m/s east who walks 1 m/s toward the front has velocity 61 m/s east relative to the ground but only 1 m/s east relative to the train. To add relative velocities: v_A relative to C = v_A relative to B + v_B relative to C.
facts.visualDescription: A person walking on a moving train with velocity arrows showing relative motion

facts.correctAnswer: Zero (no horizontal acceleration)
acceptableAlternatives: horizontal acceleration is zero
0 m/s²
facts.quizQuestion: In ideal projectile motion (no air resistance), what is the horizontal component of acceleration?
facts.explanation: With no air resistance, there is no horizontal force on a projectile, so horizontal acceleration = 0. Horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the flight. Only gravity acts, producing vertical acceleration of g ≈ 10 m/s² downward. This independence of horizontal and vertical motion is a core AP concept.
facts.visualDescription: Projectile arc with horizontal velocity arrows of equal length and vertical arrows increasing downward

facts.correctAnswer: g ≈ 10 m/s² downward
acceptableAlternatives: gravitational acceleration downward
facts.quizQuestion: In projectile motion, what is the vertical acceleration throughout the flight (near Earth's surface)?
facts.explanation: Gravity causes a constant downward acceleration of g ≈ 10 m/s² throughout projectile flight. The vertical velocity changes by 10 m/s every second (increasing downward). At the peak of its arc, the projectile has zero vertical velocity but still has this full downward acceleration.
facts.visualDescription: Projectile path with vertical velocity vectors growing downward at equal time intervals
distractors: g upward on way up, downward on way down

facts.correctAnswer: Horizontal: A cos θ; Vertical: A sin θ
acceptableAlternatives: x-component = A cosθ, y-component = A sinθ
facts.quizQuestion: For a vector A at angle θ above the horizontal, what are its x (horizontal) and y (vertical) components?
facts.explanation: A_x = A cos θ (horizontal component) and A_y = A sin θ (vertical component), where θ is measured from the positive x-axis. To reconstruct the vector: magnitude A = √(A_x² + A_y²), direction θ = arctan(A_y/A_x). These decompositions allow 2D vector problems to be solved as two independent 1D problems.
facts.visualDescription: Right triangle showing vector A, angle θ, and labeled components A_x and A_y
distractors: Horizontal: A sin θ; Vertical: A cos θ
Horizontal: A tan θ; Vertical: A cos θ
Horizontal: A/cos θ; Vertical: A/sin θ
Horizontal: A cos²θ; Vertical: A sin²θ
Horizontal: A sin²θ; Vertical: A cos²θ
Both equal A/√2 regardless of angle

facts.correctAnswer: A = √(A_x² + A_y²)
acceptableAlternatives: Pythagorean theorem applied to components
square root of sum of squared components
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the magnitude of a vector from its x and y components?
facts.explanation: The Pythagorean theorem applies to vector components: |A| = √(A_x² + A_y²). For a projectile with v_x = 30 m/s and v_y = 40 m/s, the speed = √(900 + 1600) = √2500 = 50 m/s. The direction is then θ = arctan(A_y/A_x).
facts.visualDescription: Right triangle with legs A_x and A_y and hypotenuse labeled |A|

facts.correctAnswer: Speed
acceptableAlternatives: scalar speed
magnitude of velocity
facts.quizQuestion: What scalar quantity equals the magnitude of velocity and is defined as distance traveled per unit time?
facts.explanation: Speed = distance/time, and it is always non-negative. It is the scalar counterpart to velocity (a vector). Average speed = total distance / total time — not displacement/time. A car completing a lap at constant speed but returning to start has non-zero average speed but zero average velocity.
facts.visualDescription: Speedometer gauge showing only a number with no directional indicator

facts.correctAnswer: Free fall
acceptableAlternatives: free-fall motion
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes motion under gravity alone, with no air resistance, where all objects accelerate at the same rate regardless of mass?
facts.explanation: In free fall, gravity is the only force acting. Near Earth's surface, all objects fall with the same downward acceleration g ≈ 10 m/s², regardless of mass. This was famously demonstrated by Galileo (allegedly at Pisa) and by the Apollo 15 hammer-feather experiment on the Moon. Air resistance breaks this equality.
facts.visualDescription: A heavy ball and a feather falling side by side in a vacuum tube at the same rate

facts.correctAnswer: Instantaneous velocity
acceptableAlternatives: velocity at a specific instant
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the velocity of an object at one specific moment in time, equal to the slope of the x-t curve at that point?
facts.explanation: Instantaneous velocity is the limit of Δx/Δt as Δt → 0. On a position-time graph, it equals the slope of the tangent line drawn at that specific point. Unlike average velocity (which covers an interval), instantaneous velocity captures motion at an exact instant — what a speedometer measures, with direction.
facts.visualDescription: Position-time graph with a tangent line touching at one point, labeled as instantaneous velocity

facts.correctAnswer: Opposite to the direction of motion (object is slowing down)
acceptableAlternatives: negative acceleration while moving positively
deceleration means acceleration opposes velocity
facts.quizQuestion: If an object moves in the positive direction but its acceleration points in the negative direction, what is happening to its speed?
facts.explanation: When acceleration and velocity point in opposite directions, the object slows down. Speed decreases because each moment the velocity gets a push opposite to its current direction. If acceleration continues, the object eventually stops, then moves in the negative direction. This is called deceleration or negative acceleration.
facts.visualDescription: Velocity arrow pointing right with acceleration arrow pointing left, showing decreasing speed
distractors: The object moves at constant speed
The object has zero net force
The object is in free fall

facts.correctAnswer: Range increases as launch angle approaches 45°
acceptableAlternatives: maximum range at 45 degrees launch angle
facts.quizQuestion: For a projectile launched at the same initial speed, at what launch angle is horizontal range maximized (on level ground)?
facts.explanation: For level ground and no air resistance, horizontal range R = v₀² sin(2θ)/g is maximized when sin(2θ) = 1, i.e., 2θ = 90°, so θ = 45°. Angles symmetric about 45° (e.g., 30° and 60°) yield identical ranges. The 45° result assumes equal launch and landing heights.
facts.visualDescription: Three projectile arcs at 30°, 45°, 60° showing the 45° arc reaching the farthest horizontal distance
distractors: Range is independent of launch angle

facts.correctAnswer: Position
acceptableAlternatives: x
location relative to a reference point
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the location of an object relative to a chosen reference point (origin), measured as a vector quantity?
facts.explanation: Position is a vector quantity measured from a chosen reference point (origin) to the object's location. It requires a coordinate system and can be negative (left or below origin). Displacement = change in position = x_final − x_initial. Position itself (not displacement) tells you WHERE the object is, not how far it moved.
facts.visualDescription: A number line with an origin and an object at position x, with a vector arrow from origin to object

facts.correctAnswer: Constant (uniform) acceleration
acceptableAlternatives: uniform acceleration
acceleration does not change
facts.quizQuestion: The three kinematic equations (v = v₀+at, x = x₀+v₀t+½at², v² = v₀²+2aΔx) are valid ONLY under what condition?
facts.explanation: The kinematic equations apply ONLY when acceleration is constant (uniform). If acceleration changes over time, you cannot use these equations directly — you would need calculus (integration) or graphical methods. Free fall near Earth's surface qualifies because g is essentially constant. Circular motion does NOT qualify (centripetal acceleration changes direction).
facts.visualDescription: A straight line on an a-t graph labeled 'constant acceleration' with the three kinematic equations listed

facts.correctAnswer: Horizontal and vertical motions are independent of each other
acceptableAlternatives: independence of horizontal and vertical components
facts.quizQuestion: What key principle allows projectile motion to be analyzed as two separate 1D problems?
facts.explanation: In projectile motion, horizontal and vertical motions are completely independent. Horizontal: constant velocity (no air resistance). Vertical: constant downward acceleration g. They share only time as a common variable. This means a horizontally launched ball hits the ground at the SAME TIME as a ball dropped from the same height — both fall the same vertical distance in the same time.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison of dropped ball and horizontally launched ball reaching the ground simultaneously
distractors: Horizontal and vertical motions add together
Vertical motion depends on horizontal speed
Time of flight depends on horizontal velocity
Both components have equal acceleration g
The path shape depends on initial horizontal velocity
Horizontal speed decreases due to gravity
Air resistance affects horizontal motion more than vertical

facts.correctAnswer: v_avg = (v₀ + v) / 2
acceptableAlternatives: average of initial and final velocities
facts.quizQuestion: For constant acceleration, what useful equation gives average velocity as the mean of initial and final velocities?
facts.explanation: When acceleration is constant, v_avg = (v₀ + v)/2. This makes sense because the velocity changes linearly, so the average is just the midpoint value. This is NOT valid for non-constant acceleration. It is often used alongside the kinematic equations to solve for unknowns — for example, combined with Δx = v_avg × t.
facts.visualDescription: A v-t graph with initial velocity v₀ and final velocity v, and midpoint labeled as v_avg

facts.correctAnswer: At rest and moving at constant velocity both have zero acceleration
acceptableAlternatives: constant velocity means zero acceleration
acceleration is zero for uniform motion or rest
facts.quizQuestion: What do 'at rest' and 'moving at constant velocity' have in common regarding acceleration?
facts.explanation: Both states have zero acceleration (a = 0). 'At rest' is simply the special case where velocity = 0 and a = 0. 'Constant velocity' means v ≠ 0 but a = 0. Newton's First Law covers both: in either case, the net force must be zero. A common misconception is that 'at rest' and 'in motion' are fundamentally different — they are not; both require ΣF = 0.
facts.visualDescription: Two scenarios side by side: a stationary car (a=0, v=0) and a car moving at constant highway speed (a=0, v=constant)
distractors: Both require a non-zero net force
Both require gravity to be zero
Both have the same acceleration (equal to g)
Both have a net force equal to weight
Both are examples of static equilibrium

facts.correctAnswer: Acceleration in the negative direction — could be speeding up or slowing down
acceptableAlternatives: negative acceleration depends on direction of velocity
not always slowing down
facts.quizQuestion: Does negative acceleration always mean an object is slowing down?
facts.explanation: No. Negative acceleration means acceleration in the negative direction. If the object also moves in the negative direction (negative velocity), it SPEEDS UP. Only when velocity and acceleration have opposite signs does the object slow down. A ball thrown upward has downward (negative) acceleration throughout — it slows going up, stops, then speeds up going down — all with the same acceleration.
facts.visualDescription: A ball thrown upward with a downward acceleration arrow constant throughout, showing slowing on the way up and speeding on the way down
distractors: Yes, negative acceleration always means slowing down
Negative acceleration means the object has stopped
Negative acceleration applies only to falling objects
Negative acceleration is the same as deceleration always
Negative acceleration means speed is decreasing
Negative acceleration means the object moves left
Negative acceleration only occurs in the vertical direction

facts.correctAnswer: Straight line with constant slope (slope = velocity)
acceptableAlternatives: linear x-t graph means constant velocity
facts.quizQuestion: What does the position-time (x-t) graph look like for an object moving at constant velocity?
facts.explanation: Constant velocity → constant slope of x-t graph → straight line. The slope equals the velocity (v = Δx/Δt = rise/run). A steeper slope means faster constant motion. A horizontal line (slope = 0) means the object is at rest. A curved x-t graph indicates changing velocity (non-zero acceleration). Recognizing straight vs. curved x-t graphs is an essential AP skill.
facts.visualDescription: A straight line on an x-t graph labeled 'constant slope = constant velocity'

facts.correctAnswer: Parabolic (curved) shape — indicates constant acceleration
acceptableAlternatives: curved x-t graph means changing velocity (acceleration present)
facts.quizQuestion: What shape does the position-time (x-t) graph have for an object under constant acceleration?
facts.explanation: x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at² is a quadratic in t → the x-t graph is parabolic. The curve opens upward for positive acceleration, downward for negative acceleration. The slope (velocity) changes continuously. Free-fall produces a downward-opening parabola for height vs. time (taking up as positive). Projectile motion height vs. time is also parabolic.
facts.visualDescription: A parabolic x-t graph curving upward, labeled 'constant acceleration produces parabolic x-t graph'

facts.correctAnswer: m/s (meters per second)
acceptableAlternatives: SI unit of velocity is meters per second
facts.quizQuestion: What are the SI units of velocity and speed?
facts.explanation: Velocity and speed are both measured in m/s (meters per second) in SI. Acceleration: m/s². Displacement and distance: m. Time: s. Knowing SI units helps check equations by dimensional analysis: if you compute ΔKE = ½mv² and get kg × (m/s)² = kg⋅m²/s² = J, the units confirm the formula is correct (joules).
facts.visualDescription: A speedometer labeled in m/s with the SI unit highlighted

facts.correctAnswer: v = 0 at the peak; acceleration = g downward throughout
acceptableAlternatives: zero velocity at maximum height, g downward always
facts.quizQuestion: At the peak of a vertically thrown ball's trajectory, what are its velocity and acceleration?
facts.explanation: At the peak: velocity = 0 (momentarily stopped — direction changing from upward to downward). Acceleration = g ≈ 10 m/s² DOWNWARD (unchanged throughout the entire flight — gravity doesn't 'turn off' at the peak). This is a classic AP misconception: students often say 'acceleration is zero at the peak' because velocity is zero — WRONG. A = −g continuously.
facts.visualDescription: Ball at peak height with v = 0 labeled and a downward acceleration arrow g labeled (common misconception 'a = 0 here?' crossed out)
distractors: v = 0 and a = 0 at the peak
v is maximum downward and a = g
v = g downward and a = g downward
v = 0 and a changes direction at peak
v = 0 only briefly but a switches to upward at peak

facts.correctAnswer: Time of flight depends on vertical motion only (not horizontal velocity)
acceptableAlternatives: horizontal velocity doesn't affect how long projectile stays in air
facts.quizQuestion: For a projectile launched horizontally from a cliff, what determines the time it takes to hit the ground?
facts.explanation: Time of flight depends only on vertical motion: the height h of the cliff and g. From y = ½gt²: t = √(2h/g). The horizontal velocity has ZERO effect on time of flight — a ball thrown horizontally from a cliff hits the ground at the same time as one dropped from the same height. This demonstrates the independence of horizontal and vertical motion.
facts.visualDescription: Two balls launched from a cliff — one dropped, one thrown horizontally — both hitting the ground simultaneously
distractors: Horizontal velocity determines time of flight
Both horizontal and vertical motion determine time of flight
Time of flight is always the same regardless of height
A faster horizontal launch takes longer to fall
Time depends on the launch angle only
Time of flight equals horizontal distance divided by speed
Time is determined by the angle between velocity and gravity

facts.correctAnswer: The projectile returns to the same height with the same speed but opposite vertical velocity
acceptableAlternatives: launch speed equals landing speed for symmetric trajectory
facts.quizQuestion: For a projectile launched and landing at the same height, how do the launch and landing speeds compare?
facts.explanation: By conservation of energy (or symmetry of projectile motion), the speed at landing equals the speed at launch when the projectile returns to the same height. The horizontal velocity component is unchanged (v_x constant). The vertical component v_y is equal in magnitude but opposite in direction (up on launch, down on landing). Therefore: v_final = v_initial in magnitude.
facts.visualDescription: Symmetric projectile arc showing equal speeds at launch and landing, with horizontal velocity constant and vertical velocity reversed
distractors: Landing speed is always greater due to gravity
Landing speed is less than launch speed
Speed at landing depends on horizontal distance traveled
The projectile speeds up on the way down so lands faster
Launch and landing speeds are only equal if launched at 45°
Landing speed equals launch speed only for vertical throws

facts.correctAnswer: System
acceptableAlternatives: physical system
defined set of objects
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes a defined collection of objects whose interactions and properties are being analyzed?
facts.explanation: A system is a collection of objects chosen for analysis. Everything outside the system is the 'environment.' The boundary of the system determines what forces are internal (between objects within the system) vs. external (from the environment). Choosing the right system simplifies applying Newton's laws.
facts.visualDescription: A dashed boundary enclosing two interacting blocks, labeled 'system'

facts.correctAnswer: Center of mass
acceptableAlternatives: CM
average position weighted by mass
facts.quizQuestion: What point in a system behaves as if all the system's mass were concentrated there for analyzing external forces?
facts.explanation: The center of mass (CM) is the mass-weighted average position of all objects in a system: x_cm = Σ(m_i x_i) / Σm_i. External forces on a system accelerate the CM as if all mass were there. For a symmetric uniform object, the CM is at its geometric center. The CM can be outside the object (e.g., a donut).
facts.visualDescription: A seesaw balanced at a point that's closer to the heavier mass, labeled as center of mass
distractors: Center of gravity only near Earth

facts.correctAnswer: x_cm = Σ(m_i × x_i) / Σm_i
acceptableAlternatives: sum of mass times position divided by total mass
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the x-coordinate of the center of mass for a system of particles?
facts.explanation: x_cm = Σ(m_i × x_i) / Σm_i sums the product of each mass times its position, divided by total mass. For two masses: x_cm = (m₁x₁ + m₂x₂)/(m₁+m₂). This is a mass-weighted average — heavier objects pull the CM toward them. The CM moves with constant velocity if there is no net external force.
facts.visualDescription: Two masses on a number line with the CM marker sitting closer to the heavier mass

facts.correctAnswer: Force
acceptableAlternatives: push or pull interaction between two objects
facts.quizQuestion: What vector quantity describes a push or pull interaction between two objects that can cause acceleration?
facts.explanation: A force is a vector interaction between two objects. Forces are not properties of a single object — they always involve an interaction pair. Forces can change an object's velocity (cause acceleration), change its direction of motion, or deform it. The SI unit of force is the newton (N = kg⋅m/s²).
facts.visualDescription: Two hands pushing toward each other with a force arrow labeled F

facts.correctAnswer: Free-body diagram (FBD)
acceptableAlternatives: force diagram
FBD
facts.quizQuestion: What type of diagram shows ALL forces acting ON a single object as labeled arrows, used to apply Newton's laws?
facts.explanation: A free-body diagram isolates one object and shows every external force acting on it as a vector arrow (magnitude and direction). The object is represented as a dot or box. FBDs are the first step in solving Newton's Second Law problems. Only forces ON the object are included — not forces the object exerts on others.
facts.visualDescription: A box with arrows showing weight down, normal force up, applied force right, and friction left

facts.correctAnswer: Newton's Third Law
acceptableAlternatives: action-reaction law
law of action and reaction
facts.quizQuestion: Which law states that every action force has an equal and opposite reaction force acting on the other object?
facts.explanation: Newton's Third Law: F_A on B = −F_B on A. Action-reaction pairs are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and act on DIFFERENT objects. They NEVER cancel because they act on different objects. Example: you push the floor down (action); the floor pushes you up (reaction). These forces always exist in pairs — you cannot have one without the other.
facts.visualDescription: Two objects with equal and opposite force arrows between them labeled F_AB and F_BA

facts.correctAnswer: They act on DIFFERENT objects (different agents and recipients)
acceptableAlternatives: action and reaction act on different objects
facts.quizQuestion: Why do Newton's Third Law action-reaction force pairs never cancel each other out?
facts.explanation: Forces cancel when they act on the SAME object (as in Newton's Second Law, where net force = sum of all forces on one object). But action-reaction pairs act on DIFFERENT objects — each force in the pair is on a different body. You can never add them in a single object's free-body diagram because they are on different diagrams.
facts.visualDescription: Two separate free-body diagrams: one for object A showing F_BA, one for object B showing F_AB

facts.correctAnswer: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia)
acceptableAlternatives: Newton's first law
law of inertia
facts.quizQuestion: Which law states that an object remains at rest or moves at constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force?
facts.explanation: Newton's First Law defines inertia — the tendency of objects to resist changes in their motion. If net force = 0, velocity is constant (which includes being at rest). A ball rolling on a frictionless surface would roll forever without stopping. Common misconception: objects don't need a force to keep moving — they need a net force to CHANGE their motion.
facts.visualDescription: A ball rolling in a straight line forever with 'net force = 0' label and no friction arrows

facts.correctAnswer: Inertia
acceptableAlternatives: tendency to resist change in motion
facts.quizQuestion: What property of matter causes objects to resist changes in their state of motion, and is measured by mass?
facts.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of objects to maintain their current state of motion (either at rest or moving at constant velocity). Greater mass means greater inertia — a bowling ball is harder to start moving and harder to stop than a tennis ball. Inertia is NOT a force; it is a property of matter quantified by mass.
facts.visualDescription: A large boulder and a pebble, with the boulder labeled 'more inertia' due to greater mass

facts.correctAnswer: Newton's Second Law
acceptableAlternatives: F_net = ma
net force equals mass times acceleration
facts.quizQuestion: Which law states that the net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration (ΣF = ma)?
facts.explanation: Newton's Second Law: ΣF = ma. The net force (vector sum of all forces) determines acceleration. For the same net force, larger mass means smaller acceleration (a = F/m). For the same mass, larger force means larger acceleration. This is the primary equation for solving dynamics problems in AP Physics 1.
facts.visualDescription: Equation F_net = ma displayed prominently with an arrow showing the direction of both F_net and a

facts.correctAnswer: ΣF = ma (or a = F_net / m)
acceptableAlternatives: net force equals mass times acceleration
F = ma
facts.quizQuestion: What is the mathematical statement of Newton's Second Law for a single object?
facts.explanation: ΣF = ma states that the vector sum of all forces on an object equals mass times acceleration. Both ΣF and a are vectors — they point in the same direction. In 2D, this is applied as two separate equations: ΣF_x = ma_x and ΣF_y = ma_y. The SI unit of force: 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².
facts.visualDescription: The equation ΣF = ma on a chalkboard with force arrows summing to a net force arrow

facts.correctAnswer: F_g = mg
acceptableAlternatives: weight equals mass times g
W = mg
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the gravitational force (weight) on an object near Earth's surface?
facts.explanation: F_g = mg, where m is mass in kg and g ≈ 10 m/s² near Earth's surface. Weight is a FORCE (in newtons), not the same as mass (in kg). On the Moon (g ≈ 1.6 m/s²), your mass is the same but your weight is about 1/6 as much. Weight acts downward toward Earth's center.
facts.visualDescription: A person on a scale with arrow labeled F_g = mg pointing downward

facts.correctAnswer: F_g = Gm₁m₂/r²
acceptableAlternatives: Newton's law of universal gravitation equation
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the gravitational force between any two masses separated by distance r?
facts.explanation: F_g = Gm₁m₂/r² is Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation. G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N⋅m²/kg². Gravity follows an inverse-square law: double the distance, and the force drops to ¼. The force is always attractive. This equation applies everywhere in the universe; F_g = mg is a special case near Earth's surface.
facts.visualDescription: Two spheres with gravitational force arrows pointing toward each other, labeled with m₁, m₂, and r

acceptableAlternatives: 6.674e-11 N·m²/kg²
facts.quizQuestion: What is the value of the universal gravitational constant G?
facts.explanation: G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N⋅m²/kg² is an extraordinarily small number, which is why gravity is by far the weakest fundamental force — you need enormous masses (like planets) for gravity to be noticeable. G was first measured experimentally by Henry Cavendish in 1798 using a torsion balance.
facts.visualDescription: Henry Cavendish's torsion balance apparatus for measuring G

facts.correctAnswer: f_s ≤ μ_s × F_N
acceptableAlternatives: static friction is at most mu_s times normal force
facts.quizQuestion: What inequality gives the maximum static friction force between two surfaces?
facts.explanation: Static friction f_s ≤ μ_s F_N. The ≤ sign is crucial: static friction is a RESPONSE force — it only equals μ_s F_N at the point of impending motion (maximum static friction). For smaller applied forces, f_s is smaller. Once the applied force exceeds μ_s F_N, the object begins to slide (kinetic friction takes over).
facts.visualDescription: A block on a surface with a growing applied force arrow and a static friction arrow opposing it, labeled f_s ≤ μ_s F_N

facts.correctAnswer: f_k = μ_k × F_N
acceptableAlternatives: kinetic friction equals mu_k times normal force
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the kinetic (sliding) friction force between two surfaces in relative motion?
facts.explanation: f_k = μ_k F_N, where μ_k is the coefficient of kinetic friction and F_N is the normal force. Unlike static friction, kinetic friction is a constant value (equals sign, not ≤). Kinetic friction is always less than maximum static friction (μ_k < μ_s), which is why it's easier to keep something sliding than to start it sliding.
facts.visualDescription: A block sliding on a surface with kinetic friction arrow labeled f_k = μ_k F_N opposing motion

facts.correctAnswer: Normal force
acceptableAlternatives: F_N
contact perpendicular force
facts.quizQuestion: What contact force acts perpendicular to a surface and prevents objects from passing through it?
facts.explanation: The normal force (F_N) acts perpendicular to the contact surface. It is a contact force that arises from electromagnetic repulsion between atoms. On a flat horizontal surface, F_N = mg for a stationary object; on an incline, F_N = mg cos θ. 'Normal' means perpendicular in mathematics.
facts.visualDescription: A block on a flat surface with a vertical upward arrow labeled F_N and downward weight arrow

facts.correctAnswer: Tension
acceptableAlternatives: T
pulling force through a rope or string
facts.quizQuestion: What contact force is transmitted through a string, rope, or cable, always pulling the attached object toward the connector?
facts.explanation: Tension (T) is the force transmitted along a string, rope, or cable. Ropes can only pull (tension), never push (compression). For a massless rope over a frictionless pulley, tension is the same throughout the rope. Tension always pulls the object toward the rope — away from the object's position.
facts.visualDescription: A hanging object on a string with upward tension arrow labeled T and downward weight arrow

facts.correctAnswer: F_s = −kx (Hooke's Law)
acceptableAlternatives: spring force equals negative k times displacement
F = -kx
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the restoring force of a spring, where k is the spring constant and x is displacement from equilibrium?
facts.explanation: Hooke's Law: F_s = −kx. The negative sign indicates the force is RESTORING — it opposes the displacement. Stretch the spring right (+x), the spring pulls left (−F). Compress left (−x), the spring pushes right (+F). The spring constant k (in N/m) measures stiffness — larger k means stiffer spring.
facts.visualDescription: A spring with equilibrium position marked, showing displacement x and restoring force arrow pointing back toward equilibrium

facts.correctAnswer: Spring constant (k), measured in N/m
acceptableAlternatives: k
force constant
facts.quizQuestion: What quantity in Hooke's Law measures how stiff a spring is, with SI units of N/m?
facts.explanation: The spring constant k (also called force constant) indicates spring stiffness. A large k means the spring requires more force per meter of stretch. Units: N/m (newtons per meter). Example: k = 200 N/m means a 200 N force is required to stretch the spring 1 meter. Springs in series have smaller effective k; springs in parallel have larger effective k.
facts.visualDescription: Two springs side by side — a stiff one (large k, barely stretched) and a soft one (small k, greatly stretched)

facts.correctAnswer: a_c = v²/r (directed toward the center)
acceptableAlternatives: centripetal acceleration equals v squared over r
v²/r toward center
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the centripetal acceleration of an object in uniform circular motion?
facts.explanation: a_c = v²/r, always directed toward the center of the circle. Even though speed is constant in uniform circular motion, velocity (a vector) constantly changes direction — hence there IS acceleration (centripetal). Larger speed or smaller radius increases centripetal acceleration. Also written as a_c = ω²r.
facts.visualDescription: A ball on a circular path with a centripetal acceleration arrow pointing toward the center at every position

facts.correctAnswer: Centripetal force is NOT a separate force — it is the net inward force
acceptableAlternatives: centripetal force is the net force directed toward center
not a new type of force
facts.quizQuestion: Why is 'centripetal force' never drawn as a separate arrow on a free-body diagram?
facts.explanation: Centripetal force is NOT a new type of force — it is the label for whatever net force happens to point toward the center of circular motion. For a car turning, friction provides the centripetal force. For a satellite, gravity provides it. For a ball on a string, tension provides it. Drawing 'F_c' as an extra arrow on an FBD would double-count forces.
facts.visualDescription: A free-body diagram of a car turning with only friction labeled (not a separate centripetal force arrow)
distractors: Centripetal force is gravity in disguise
Centripetal force is an outward reaction force
Centripetal force is the normal force only

facts.correctAnswer: F_c = mv²/r
acceptableAlternatives: centripetal force equals m v-squared over r
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the net inward (centripetal) force required to keep an object in uniform circular motion?
facts.explanation: F_c = mv²/r, derived from F = ma and a_c = v²/r. This represents the net force that MUST exist toward the center for circular motion to occur. If this inward force disappears (e.g., string breaks), the object flies off in a straight line tangent to the circle (Newton's First Law). Also: F_c = mω²r.
facts.visualDescription: A ball on a string in circular motion with force arrows showing tension toward center equaling mv²/r

facts.correctAnswer: Mass is amount of matter (kg); weight is gravitational force (N)
acceptableAlternatives: mass is scalar in kg; weight is force in newtons
facts.quizQuestion: What is the key distinction between mass and weight?
facts.explanation: Mass (m) measures the amount of matter in kg — it does not change with location. Weight (F_g = mg) is the gravitational force in newtons — it changes with g. An astronaut has the same mass on the Moon as on Earth, but their weight is about 1/6 as much. Mass is a scalar; weight is a vector (downward).
facts.visualDescription: A scale showing different weight readings on Earth vs. Moon with the same object (same mass, different weight)

acceptableAlternatives: weight component along incline is mg sinθ, perpendicular is mg cosθ
facts.quizQuestion: For an object on an incline at angle θ, what are the components of gravity parallel and perpendicular to the incline surface?
facts.explanation: On an incline at angle θ: The component of gravity along the incline (pulling the object down the slope) = mg sin θ. The component perpendicular to the incline (pressing into the surface) = mg cos θ. The normal force equals mg cos θ (since there's no acceleration perpendicular to the incline). Larger θ means more force pulling down the slope.
facts.visualDescription: An incline with angle θ, showing the weight vector decomposed into parallel (mg sinθ) and perpendicular (mg cosθ) components

facts.correctAnswer: Gravity follows an inverse-square law with distance
acceptableAlternatives: gravitational force decreases as 1/r²
facts.quizQuestion: How does the gravitational force between two objects change if the distance between them is doubled?
facts.explanation: From F_g = Gm₁m₂/r², doubling the distance (2r) gives F_new = Gm₁m₂/(2r)² = Gm₁m₂/4r² = F_original/4. The force drops to one-quarter. This inverse-square relationship means gravity falls off rapidly with distance but never reaches zero — it is technically infinite in range.
facts.visualDescription: Graph of F_g vs. r showing a curve that decreases steeply as r increases, labeled 1/r²

facts.correctAnswer: Coefficient of friction (μ)
acceptableAlternatives: friction coefficient
μ
facts.quizQuestion: What dimensionless number represents the ratio of friction force to normal force, depending on the materials in contact?
facts.explanation: The coefficient of friction (μ) is dimensionless and depends on the material pair (rubber on concrete vs. ice on steel). Static coefficient μ_s ≥ kinetic coefficient μ_k for any given pair. μ = 0 means frictionless; μ = 1 means friction force equals normal force. Typical values: μ_s for rubber on concrete ≈ 0.7–0.8.
facts.visualDescription: A table showing μ_s and μ_k values for different material pairs like rubber/concrete and ice/steel

facts.correctAnswer: Newton (N) = kg⋅m/s²
acceptableAlternatives: 1 newton equals 1 kilogram meter per second squared
facts.quizQuestion: What is the SI unit of force, and how is it expressed in base units?
facts.explanation: 1 newton (N) = 1 kg⋅m/s², derived from Newton's Second Law: F = ma → kg × m/s² = N. Named after Isaac Newton. A newton is roughly the weight of a 100 g apple (F = 0.1 kg × 10 m/s² = 1 N). All force quantities in physics should be expressed in newtons in SI.
facts.visualDescription: An apple (~100g) hanging from a spring scale reading 1 N

facts.correctAnswer: Atwood machine: a = (m₂−m₁)g/(m₁+m₂)
acceptableAlternatives: unbalanced masses on a pulley accelerate with net force over total mass
facts.quizQuestion: For an Atwood machine (two unequal masses m₁ < m₂ connected over a frictionless pulley), what is the system's acceleration?
facts.explanation: a = (m₂−m₁)g/(m₁+m₂). Derived by treating both masses as one system: net force = (m₂−m₁)g (only the difference in weight is unbalanced), total mass = m₁+m₂. The tension T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂). If m₁ = m₂, a = 0 (equilibrium). This classic device is used to measure g with reduced effective force.
facts.visualDescription: Two masses hanging from opposite ends of a string over a pulley, with m₂ > m₁ causing downward acceleration on the m₂ side

facts.correctAnswer: Net force equals zero (object is in translational equilibrium)
acceptableAlternatives: ΣF = 0
equilibrium: no acceleration
facts.quizQuestion: If an object moves at constant velocity (including being at rest), what must be true about the net force acting on it?
facts.explanation: Newton's First Law: constant velocity (Δv = 0) means acceleration = 0, which requires ΣF = 0. This does NOT mean no forces act — it means all forces BALANCE. A book on a desk has gravity down and normal force up, both equal and opposite, summing to zero. The object is in translational equilibrium.
facts.visualDescription: A book on a table with weight down and normal force up of equal magnitude, labeled ΣF = 0

facts.quizQuestion: In which direction does friction act relative to an object's motion?
facts.explanation: Friction always opposes relative motion between surfaces. Kinetic friction opposes the direction of actual sliding. Static friction opposes the tendency of motion — it adjusts direction as needed to prevent sliding. On an inclined plane, friction acts up the slope (opposing the tendency to slide down). Friction can act in any direction — it doesn't have a fixed absolute direction.

facts.quizQuestion: Why is the minus sign in Hooke's Law (F = −kx) significant?
facts.explanation: The negative sign indicates the force is RESTORING — it acts opposite to the displacement from equilibrium. Stretch the spring (positive x) → force is negative (pulls back). Compress (negative x) → force is positive (pushes out). Without the restoring nature, there would be no oscillation. This is the defining feature that leads to SHM.
facts.visualDescription: A spring stretched to the right showing the restoring force arrow pointing left, and compressed showing force pointing right

facts.correctAnswer: T = 2πr/v (or T = 2π/ω)
acceptableAlternatives: period equals circumference divided by speed
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the period of uniform circular motion for an object moving at speed v on a circle of radius r?
facts.explanation: T = 2πr/v: the circumference (2πr) divided by the speed gives the time for one full revolution. Equivalently, T = 2π/ω. For satellites: combining GMm/r² = mv²/r gives v = √(GM/r), so T = 2πr/v = 2πr^(3/2)/√(GM). This is the basis of Kepler's Third Law.
facts.visualDescription: A circle of radius r with an arrow showing the object traversing the circumference 2πr at speed v

facts.correctAnswer: F_N = mg cos θ
acceptableAlternatives: normal force on incline equals mg cosine theta
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the normal force on an object at rest on a frictionless inclined plane at angle θ?
facts.explanation: F_N = mg cos θ because the normal force must balance the perpendicular component of gravity. As θ increases (steeper incline), cos θ decreases, so F_N decreases. At θ = 0° (flat surface), F_N = mg. At θ = 90° (vertical wall), F_N = 0. This also affects kinetic friction: f_k = μ_k × mg cos θ.
facts.visualDescription: A block on an incline at angle θ with the normal force arrow perpendicular to the surface and labeled mg cos θ
distractors: F_N = mg sin θ cos θ

facts.correctAnswer: Near-surface: F_g = mg; Universal: F_g = Gm₁m₂/r²
acceptableAlternatives: mg is valid near Earth's surface only; universal law works anywhere
facts.quizQuestion: When is it appropriate to use F_g = mg vs. F_g = Gm₁m₂/r² for gravitational force?
facts.explanation: F_g = mg is a simplified version valid near Earth's surface where g is essentially constant (within a few hundred km). F_g = Gm₁m₂/r² is the exact universal law valid anywhere — for satellites, planets, and stars. The near-surface approximation comes from setting g = GM_Earth/R_Earth² ≈ 9.8 m/s² (≈ 10 m/s² for AP).
facts.visualDescription: Two scenarios: a person on Earth's surface using mg, and a satellite in orbit using Gm₁m₂/r²

facts.quizQuestion: For an ideal (massless, frictionless) pulley system with a single rope, how does tension vary along the rope?
facts.explanation: For a massless rope over a frictionless pulley, the tension is the SAME throughout the entire rope. This is because any difference in tension would create a net force on a massless rope, giving it infinite acceleration — impossible. With a massive rope or friction, tension varies. This assumption simplifies Atwood machine and inclined plane problems.

facts.correctAnswer: Kinetic energy
acceptableAlternatives: KE
translational kinetic energy
facts.quizQuestion: What form of energy does an object possess due to its translational motion, equal to ½mv²?
facts.explanation: Kinetic energy K = ½mv². It is a scalar quantity measured in joules (J). Kinetic energy is always non-negative (v² ≥ 0). Doubling speed quadruples kinetic energy (since v²). KE depends on the reference frame — an object at rest has KE = 0 in that frame but may have KE in other frames.
facts.visualDescription: A speeding car with the equation K = ½mv² overlaid, showing increasing KE with speed

facts.correctAnswer: K = ½mv²
acceptableAlternatives: KE = one-half m v-squared
½mv²
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the translational kinetic energy of an object with mass m and speed v?
facts.explanation: K = ½mv². Units: ½ × kg × (m/s)² = kg⋅m²/s² = J (joules). The ½ factor comes from integrating F = ma over distance (the work-energy derivation). For a 2 kg ball at 3 m/s: K = ½(2)(9) = 9 J. Kinetic energy is frame-dependent but always non-negative.
facts.visualDescription: A rolling ball labeled m with velocity v, and the equation K = ½mv² beside it

facts.correctAnswer: Work
acceptableAlternatives: mechanical work
W
facts.quizQuestion: What scalar quantity measures the energy transfer to or from an object by a force acting over a displacement?
facts.explanation: Work (W) = Fd cos θ, where θ is the angle between the force and displacement vectors. Work is a scalar measured in joules. Positive work adds energy to the object; negative work removes energy. Zero work is done when force is perpendicular to displacement (e.g., carrying a box horizontally — normal force does no work because gravity is perpendicular to horizontal displacement).
facts.visualDescription: A force arrow at angle θ to a horizontal displacement arrow, with W = Fd cos θ labeled

facts.correctAnswer: W = Fd cos θ
acceptableAlternatives: work equals force times displacement times cosine of angle between them
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the work done by a constant force F on an object that undergoes displacement d?
facts.explanation: W = Fd cos θ, where θ is the angle between F and d. When F and d are parallel (θ = 0°): W = Fd (maximum positive work). When perpendicular (θ = 90°): cos 90° = 0, so W = 0. When antiparallel (θ = 180°): W = −Fd (negative work, removing energy). SI unit: 1 J = 1 N⋅m.
facts.visualDescription: A box being pushed at angle θ to horizontal with displacement d, labeled W = Fd cos θ

facts.correctAnswer: Work-energy theorem: W_net = ΔK
acceptableAlternatives: net work equals change in kinetic energy
W_net = K_f − K_i
facts.quizQuestion: What theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy?
facts.explanation: Work-energy theorem: W_net = ΔK = K_f − K_i. This connects dynamics (forces doing work) to kinematics (change in speed). If net work is positive, KE increases (object speeds up). If negative, KE decreases (object slows). This theorem holds for any path and any force configuration as long as you use the NET work.
facts.visualDescription: A bar chart showing initial KE + net work = final KE

facts.correctAnswer: U_g = mgh
acceptableAlternatives: gravitational PE equals mgh
gravitational potential energy = mgh
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the gravitational potential energy of an object near Earth's surface?
facts.explanation: U_g = mgh, where h is the height above a chosen reference level (usually the ground). This is a RELATIVE quantity — you must define h = 0 somewhere. The object stored energy by being raised against gravity. When it falls, U_g decreases and K increases. Valid only near Earth's surface where g is constant.
facts.visualDescription: A ball at height h above the ground with the equation U = mgh labeled, and a reference level marked at h = 0

facts.correctAnswer: U_s = ½kx²
acceptableAlternatives: elastic potential energy equals half k x squared
spring PE = ½kx²
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the elastic potential energy stored in a spring compressed or stretched by displacement x?
facts.explanation: U_s = ½kx², where k is the spring constant (N/m) and x is the displacement from equilibrium. The ½ arises because the average force over the stretch is ½kx (force increases linearly from 0 to kx). Doubling the stretch quadruples the stored energy. At maximum compression/extension in SHM, all energy is elastic PE.
facts.visualDescription: A compressed spring with displacement x labeled and the equation U = ½kx² shown

facts.correctAnswer: Conservation of mechanical energy: E_total = K + U = constant
acceptableAlternatives: total mechanical energy is conserved in isolated system
K_i + U_i = K_f + U_f
facts.quizQuestion: What law states that the total mechanical energy (kinetic + potential) remains constant in an isolated system with no nonconservative forces?
facts.explanation: Conservation of mechanical energy: K_i + U_i = K_f + U_f, valid when only conservative forces do work (gravity, spring). If friction or other nonconservative forces act, mechanical energy is NOT conserved — it converts to thermal energy. Energy is always conserved in total, but mechanical energy specifically can decrease.
facts.visualDescription: A pendulum at three positions showing energy bar charts: all PE at the top, all KE at the bottom
distractors: Newton's Second Law in energy form

facts.correctAnswer: K_i + U_i = K_f + U_f
acceptableAlternatives: initial KE plus initial PE equals final KE plus final PE
facts.quizQuestion: For a system where only conservative forces do work, what equation expresses conservation of mechanical energy?
facts.explanation: K_i + U_i = K_f + U_f. When friction is present, the equation becomes K_i + U_i = K_f + U_f + W_friction (where W_friction > 0 is energy lost to heat). The total energy including thermal energy is still conserved, but we say mechanical energy is 'lost' to nonconservative forces. This equation is the workhorse of Unit 3 problems.
facts.visualDescription: Energy bar chart at two moments showing K + U equal on both sides

facts.correctAnswer: Power

rate of doing work
facts.quizQuestion: What scalar quantity measures the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts?
facts.explanation: Power P = W/Δt = ΔE/Δt. It measures how quickly energy is transferred or work is done. Also P = Fv (force times velocity) for a constant force. SI unit: 1 watt (W) = 1 J/s. A 100 W lightbulb converts 100 joules of electrical energy to light and heat every second. Horsepower is an older unit: 1 hp ≈ 746 W.
facts.visualDescription: A lightbulb labeled 100 W with the equation P = W/t showing 100 J per second
