P = Fv for constant force
facts.quizQuestion: What two equivalent equations give power in terms of work/time and force/velocity?
facts.explanation: P = W/Δt = Fv. The second form (P = Fv) comes from substituting W = Fd and d/t = v. P = Fv is useful when force and velocity are known directly. For example, a car engine providing 5000 N of force at 20 m/s outputs P = (5000)(20) = 100,000 W = 100 kW.
facts.visualDescription: A race car with force and velocity arrows, labeled P = Fv = 100 kW
distractors: P = W × Δt and P = Fv²
P = ΔE × t and P = Fv

facts.correctAnswer: Watt (W) = 1 joule per second (J/s)
acceptableAlternatives: 1 watt equals 1 joule/second
W = J/s
facts.quizQuestion: What is the SI unit of power, and how is it defined in base units?
facts.explanation: 1 watt (W) = 1 J/s = 1 kg⋅m²/s³. Named after James Watt, the Scottish inventor who improved the steam engine. One watt is a very small amount of power — a typical human body at rest produces about 80 W, and a car engine produces 100,000+ W (100+ kW).
facts.visualDescription: A battery labeled '100 W' with the conversion 1 W = 1 J/s displayed

facts.correctAnswer: Joule (J) = kg⋅m²/s² = N⋅m
acceptableAlternatives: 1 joule equals 1 newton-meter
J = N·m
facts.quizQuestion: What is the SI unit of energy and work, expressed in base SI units?
facts.explanation: 1 joule (J) = 1 N⋅m = 1 kg⋅m²/s². Named after James Prescott Joule. One joule is the work done by a 1 N force over 1 meter. Kinetic energy ½mv² gives kg × (m/s)² = kg⋅m²/s² = J. All forms of energy (kinetic, potential, thermal, electrical) use joules.
facts.visualDescription: The equation J = N·m = kg·m²/s² on a chalkboard

facts.correctAnswer: Conservative force
acceptableAlternatives: path-independent force
facts.quizQuestion: What type of force does work that is independent of path, only depending on start and end positions, and is associated with potential energy?
facts.explanation: Conservative forces (gravity, spring) have two key properties: (1) work done is path-independent — only depends on endpoints, and (2) they can be associated with a potential energy. Round-trip work = 0 for conservative forces. Nonconservative forces (friction, air resistance) are path-dependent and convert mechanical energy to thermal energy.
facts.visualDescription: Three different paths between two points with equal work labeled for each, showing path independence

facts.correctAnswer: Nonconservative force (e.g., friction)
acceptableAlternatives: friction is a nonconservative force
path-dependent force
facts.quizQuestion: What type of force does work that depends on the path taken, converting mechanical energy into thermal energy and reducing total mechanical energy?
facts.explanation: Nonconservative forces (friction, air resistance, applied forces from motors) are path-dependent. Friction always opposes motion, so going a longer path means more friction work and more energy lost to heat. When friction acts, K_i + U_i ≠ K_f + U_f — mechanical energy decreases. Total energy (including thermal) is still conserved.
facts.visualDescription: A block sliding across a rough floor with thermal energy symbols (heat waves) showing energy converted to heat

facts.correctAnswer: K_i + U_i = K_f + U_f + |W_friction|
acceptableAlternatives: mechanical energy decreases by work done by friction
facts.quizQuestion: When friction does work on a system, how is the conservation of energy equation modified?
facts.explanation: K_i + U_i = K_f + U_f + |W_friction|. The work done by friction equals f_k × d and represents mechanical energy converted to thermal energy. The total energy (mechanical + thermal) is still conserved. On AP exams, this often appears as: 'A block slides down a ramp with friction — how much energy is lost to heat?'
facts.visualDescription: Energy bar chart showing initial K + U splitting into final K + U + thermal energy

facts.correctAnswer: Zero (W = Fd cos 90° = 0)
acceptableAlternatives: no work done when force perpendicular to displacement
0 joules
facts.quizQuestion: How much work does a force do if it acts perpendicular to the object's displacement?
facts.explanation: W = Fd cos θ. When θ = 90°, cos 90° = 0, so W = 0. Examples: the normal force does no work on a horizontally sliding block (force up, motion horizontal). Centripetal force does no work (always perpendicular to velocity). Carrying a box horizontally — your upward support force does no work because displacement is horizontal.
facts.visualDescription: A block moving horizontally with a vertical normal force arrow showing 90° angle between force and displacement
distractors: Work depends only on force magnitude

facts.correctAnswer: Potential energy
acceptableAlternatives: U
stored energy due to position or configuration
facts.quizQuestion: What form of energy is stored in a system due to the relative positions or configuration of its parts, associated with conservative forces?
facts.explanation: Potential energy (U) is energy stored by virtue of position or configuration. Gravitational PE (U = mgh) depends on height; elastic PE (U = ½kx²) depends on spring compression/extension. PE can be converted to KE and vice versa. PE is always measured relative to a reference level — only changes in PE are physically meaningful.
facts.visualDescription: A ball held at height h above ground and a compressed spring, both labeled with their PE stored

facts.correctAnswer: P = Fv
acceptableAlternatives: power equals force times velocity
facts.quizQuestion: For an object moving at velocity v under a constant net force F, what equation gives the instantaneous power delivered?
facts.explanation: P = Fv comes from P = W/t = Fd/t = F × (d/t) = Fv. This applies when F and v are in the same direction. If F and v are at angle θ, then P = Fv cos θ. Useful when speed is known: a car engine pushing 4000 N at 25 m/s delivers P = (4000)(25) = 100,000 W = 100 kW.
facts.visualDescription: A car engine with force arrow and velocity arrow, labeled P = Fv

facts.correctAnswer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted between forms
acceptableAlternatives: total energy of an isolated system is constant
facts.quizQuestion: What is the fundamental statement of conservation of energy?
facts.explanation: Energy is conserved: it can be converted between forms (KE ↔ PE ↔ thermal ↔ chemical ↔ etc.) but the total never increases or decreases in an isolated system. When friction 'destroys' mechanical energy, it actually converts it to thermal energy — total energy is unchanged. This is one of the most fundamental principles in all of physics.
facts.visualDescription: An energy pie chart that stays the same size while slices (KE, PE, thermal) change proportions
distractors: Energy is created when work is done
Kinetic energy is always conserved in collisions
Energy decreases over time in all systems
Potential energy is the fundamental energy form
Energy is conserved only for conservative forces
Mechanical energy equals total energy always
Energy is created by springs and gravity

facts.correctAnswer: W_gravity = mgh (positive when object descends height h)
acceptableAlternatives: work by gravity = mgh downward
facts.quizQuestion: What is the work done by gravity on an object that descends a vertical height h?
facts.explanation: W_gravity = mgh (positive for descent). When an object falls, gravity (downward) acts in the same direction as displacement (downward), so W = Fd cos 0° = mgΔh = mgh > 0. When rising, gravity does negative work: W = −mgh. This is why gravitational PE = mgh: the PE decreases (ΔU = −W_gravity) as the object descends.
facts.visualDescription: A ball falling height h with gravity arrow and displacement both pointing down, showing positive work W = mgh
distractors: W_gravity = mgh only when falling vertically

facts.correctAnswer: The object's kinetic energy increases (it speeds up)
acceptableAlternatives: positive net work increases KE
object accelerates
facts.quizQuestion: According to the work-energy theorem, what happens to an object's motion when positive net work is done on it?
facts.explanation: W_net = ΔK. Positive net work → ΔK > 0 → KE increases → speed increases. Negative net work → KE decreases → speed decreases. Zero net work → KE unchanged → constant speed. The work-energy theorem beautifully connects forces (work) to motion (KE) without needing to solve for time.
facts.visualDescription: A before/after KE bar chart with a positive 'net work' bar added, showing KE_final > KE_initial
distractors: The object's direction of motion changes
The object gains PE equal to the work done
The object moves in a circle
Work has no effect on motion

facts.correctAnswer: W_spring = −ΔU_s = −½kx_f² + ½kx_i²
acceptableAlternatives: work done by spring equals decrease in spring PE
facts.quizQuestion: What is the work done by a spring force as the spring goes from compression/extension x_i to x_f?
facts.explanation: W_spring = ½kx_i² − ½kx_f² = −ΔU_s. When a compressed spring (large U) releases to equilibrium (U = 0), it does positive work, converting PE to KE. This is why spring-powered mechanisms store energy. A spring stretched from 0 to x does −½kx² of work on the object doing the stretching (the spring stores this energy as PE).
facts.visualDescription: A compressed spring releasing and accelerating a mass, showing U_s → KE conversion
distractors: W_spring = F × Δx only at equilibrium

facts.correctAnswer: Efficiency = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%
acceptableAlternatives: e = W_out/W_in × 100%
useful output divided by total input
facts.quizQuestion: How is the efficiency of an energy conversion process defined?
facts.explanation: Efficiency = (useful energy out / total energy in) × 100%. Always ≤ 100% due to second law of thermodynamics — some energy always converts to less useful forms (usually heat). A car engine is typically 20-35% efficient; LED bulbs are ~90% efficient; a pulley system might be 80% efficient. Perfect efficiency (100%) is impossible for heat engines.
facts.visualDescription: A machine with input energy arrow and two output arrows: useful work and wasted heat, with efficiency = useful/total

facts.correctAnswer: Energy bar charts (LOL diagrams) show distribution of K, U, and work
acceptableAlternatives: bar charts showing kinetic, potential, and work-energy accounting
facts.quizQuestion: What tool in AP Physics 1 represents the energy accounting of a system at different moments using bars for K, U_g, U_s, and W?
facts.explanation: Energy bar charts (sometimes called LOL diagrams) visually show how energy is distributed at different moments. Each bar represents an energy type: K (kinetic), U_g (gravitational PE), U_s (elastic PE), and a 'W' bar for work done by external forces. The total height of all bars must remain constant for isolated systems. Used heavily on AP free-response questions.
facts.visualDescription: Two side-by-side bar charts showing energy distribution at two moments, with equal total heights

facts.correctAnswer: Linear momentum
acceptableAlternatives: p = mv
momentum
facts.quizQuestion: What vector quantity is defined as mass times velocity (p = mv) and measures an object's quantity of motion?
facts.explanation: Linear momentum p = mv is a vector quantity with the same direction as velocity. SI unit: kg⋅m/s. A heavier or faster object has more momentum. Momentum is the key quantity in collision analysis. Unlike kinetic energy (½mv²), momentum is directly proportional to v — doubling speed doubles momentum but quadruples KE.
facts.visualDescription: A fast, light ball and a slow, heavy ball shown with equal momentum arrows (p = mv for each)

facts.correctAnswer: p = mv
acceptableAlternatives: momentum equals mass times velocity
facts.quizQuestion: What equation defines linear momentum p in terms of mass m and velocity v?
facts.explanation: p = mv. Momentum is a vector (direction = direction of v). Units: kg⋅m/s. For a 0.5 kg ball at 10 m/s east: p = 0.5 × 10 = 5 kg⋅m/s east. Newton's Second Law can be written as F_net = Δp/Δt, showing force equals the rate of change of momentum — the more fundamental formulation.
facts.visualDescription: Equation p = mv with a ball moving with velocity v and mass m labeled

facts.correctAnswer: Impulse
acceptableAlternatives: J = FΔt
change in momentum
facts.quizQuestion: What vector quantity equals force multiplied by the time interval over which it acts, and equals the change in momentum?
facts.explanation: Impulse J = FΔt = Δp. It is a vector quantity with units of N⋅s = kg⋅m/s (same units as momentum). The impulse-momentum theorem states that the impulse exerted on an object equals its change in momentum. Spreading a force over a longer time reduces the peak force needed to achieve the same momentum change (car airbags, padding in helmets).
facts.visualDescription: A bat hitting a ball with a force arrow labeled F over time interval Δt, showing J = FΔt = Δp

facts.correctAnswer: Impulse-momentum theorem: J = FΔt = Δp
acceptableAlternatives: impulse equals change in momentum
FΔt = Δp
facts.quizQuestion: What theorem states that the impulse delivered to an object equals its change in momentum?
facts.explanation: J = FΔt = Δp = m(v_f − v_i). This follows from Newton's Second Law: F = ma = m(Δv/Δt) → FΔt = mΔv = Δp. The area under an F-t graph equals impulse (and thus change in momentum). This theorem explains why following through in sports and safety equipment (airbags, padding) work — they increase Δt to reduce peak F.
facts.visualDescription: Force-time graph with shaded area labeled 'J = FΔt = Δp'

facts.correctAnswer: Conservation of linear momentum
acceptableAlternatives: p_total,i = p_total,f
total momentum is conserved
facts.quizQuestion: What law states that the total momentum of a system remains constant when no external net force acts on it?
facts.explanation: Conservation of momentum: p_total,i = p_total,f when ΣF_external = 0. This follows from Newton's Third Law: internal forces in a system are equal and opposite, so they cancel in the total. It applies to ALL collisions (elastic and inelastic). Momentum is always conserved; kinetic energy may or may not be.
facts.visualDescription: Two carts colliding with momentum arrows before and after, showing total momentum unchanged

facts.correctAnswer: m₁v₁ᵢ + m₂v₂ᵢ = m₁v₁f + m₂v₂f
acceptableAlternatives: total initial momentum equals total final momentum
facts.quizQuestion: What equation expresses conservation of momentum for a two-object collision?
facts.explanation: m₁v₁ᵢ + m₂v₂ᵢ = m₁v₁f + m₂v₂f. This is a vector equation — apply it in each direction separately for 2D collisions. The key condition: no external net force (or impulse from external forces is negligible during the collision). This one equation applies to ALL collisions regardless of type.
facts.visualDescription: Two balls before and after collision with velocity arrows and the momentum equation labeled

facts.correctAnswer: Elastic collision
acceptableAlternatives: elastic
collision where kinetic energy is conserved
facts.quizQuestion: What type of collision conserves BOTH momentum AND kinetic energy?
facts.explanation: In an elastic collision, both total momentum AND total kinetic energy are conserved. Billiard balls approximate elastic collisions. In a perfectly elastic head-on collision between equal masses, the first ball stops and the second moves with the first ball's original velocity. Truly elastic collisions are rare in macroscopic objects but occur at the atomic/molecular scale.
facts.visualDescription: Two identical billiard balls: before (one moving, one at rest) and after (first stops, second moves at same speed)

facts.correctAnswer: Inelastic collision
acceptableAlternatives: inelastic
collision where kinetic energy is NOT conserved
facts.quizQuestion: What type of collision conserves momentum but NOT kinetic energy, with some KE converted to other forms?
facts.explanation: In an inelastic collision, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not — some KE becomes thermal energy, sound, or deformation energy. Most real-world collisions are inelastic. A car crash is inelastic: crumpling converts KE to deformation. The lost KE equals the increase in thermal/internal energy of the system.
facts.visualDescription: Two clay balls colliding with dented surfaces and heat symbols showing energy lost as thermal energy

facts.correctAnswer: Perfectly inelastic collision
acceptableAlternatives: completely inelastic
objects stick together after collision
facts.quizQuestion: What special type of collision results in the objects sticking together with a common final velocity, with maximum kinetic energy loss?
facts.explanation: In a perfectly inelastic collision, objects stick together: m₁v₁ᵢ + m₂v₂ᵢ = (m₁+m₂)v_f. Momentum is conserved. This represents the MAXIMUM possible loss of kinetic energy consistent with momentum conservation. Examples: bullet embedding in a block, football tackle, train cars coupling. Not all KE is lost — some remains as KE of the combined object.
facts.visualDescription: Two clay balls (one stationary, one moving) combining after collision into one mass moving slowly

facts.correctAnswer: m₁v₁ᵢ + m₂v₂ᵢ = (m₁ + m₂)v_f
acceptableAlternatives: combined mass times final velocity
objects merge: total momentum = total mass times vf
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives conservation of momentum for a perfectly inelastic collision where the objects stick together?
facts.explanation: m₁v₁ᵢ + m₂v₂ᵢ = (m₁+m₂)v_f. After they stick, there is only one final velocity. Solve for v_f: v_f = (m₁v₁ᵢ + m₂v₂ᵢ)/(m₁+m₂). If m₂ starts at rest: v_f = m₁v₁ᵢ/(m₁+m₂). The final KE is always less than initial KE (KE lost = ΔKE = ½μv_rel² where μ is reduced mass).
facts.visualDescription: Before: two separate masses with velocities. After: merged mass with velocity v_f

facts.correctAnswer: Impulse (J = Δp)
acceptableAlternatives: change in momentum
area under F-t graph equals impulse
facts.quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the area under a force-time (F-t) graph represent?
facts.explanation: Area under F-t graph = ∫F dt = impulse = Δp. This is the graphical interpretation of J = FΔt for constant forces or ∫F dt for variable forces. A triangular F-t spike (like a ball bouncing) has area = ½ × F_max × Δt = impulse. This relationship lets you find momentum change even when F varies over time.
facts.visualDescription: A force-time graph with a triangular peak, shaded area labeled 'impulse = Δp'

facts.correctAnswer: F_net = Δp/Δt
acceptableAlternatives: net force equals rate of change of momentum
facts.quizQuestion: How can Newton's Second Law be expressed in terms of momentum rather than acceleration?
facts.explanation: F_net = Δp/Δt = d(mv)/dt. This is Newton's original formulation — more general than F = ma because it allows for changing mass. For constant mass: F = m(Δv/Δt) = ma. This form directly connects force to the rate of momentum change, and integrating gives the impulse-momentum theorem: FΔt = Δp.
facts.visualDescription: Equation F_net = Δp/Δt alongside F = ma, showing they are equivalent for constant mass

facts.correctAnswer: Momentum is still conserved (total = 0 if initially at rest)
acceptableAlternatives: conservation of momentum applies to explosions too
facts.quizQuestion: A firecracker at rest explodes into two fragments. What can be said about the total momentum of the system after the explosion?
facts.explanation: Conservation of momentum applies to explosions just like collisions — there is no external net force (ignoring gravity and air resistance during the brief explosion). If the firecracker starts at rest (p_total,i = 0), then p_total,f = 0. The two fragments fly apart with equal and opposite momenta: m₁v₁ = −m₂v₂. This is Newton's Third Law in disguise.
facts.visualDescription: A firecracker at rest exploding into two fragments flying in opposite directions with equal momenta
distractors: Momentum is not conserved in explosions
Total momentum equals the explosion energy
Total KE is conserved in explosions
Momentum conservation only applies to collisions
The total momentum equals the force of the explosion

facts.correctAnswer: kg⋅m/s (same as N⋅s)
acceptableAlternatives: kilogram meters per second
newton-seconds
facts.quizQuestion: What are the SI units of both momentum and impulse?
facts.explanation: Momentum p = mv has units of kg⋅m/s. Impulse J = FΔt has units of N⋅s = kg⋅m/s²⋅s = kg⋅m/s. Both have identical units, confirming J = Δp. A 0.1 kg ball at 5 m/s has momentum 0.5 kg⋅m/s. A force of 100 N applied for 0.005 s gives impulse 0.5 N⋅s = 0.5 kg⋅m/s.
facts.visualDescription: Equations p = mv and J = FΔt with unit conversions showing both equal kg⋅m/s

facts.correctAnswer: Both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved
acceptableAlternatives: elastic collision conserves both p and KE
facts.quizQuestion: In an elastic collision, what quantities are conserved?
facts.explanation: Elastic collisions conserve both: (1) total momentum (p_total,i = p_total,f) AND (2) total kinetic energy (KE_total,i = KE_total,f). This gives two equations for a two-body collision, which (in 1D) fully determines both final velocities. For equal masses in an elastic collision: the velocities simply exchange — the moving object stops and the stationary one moves.
facts.visualDescription: Billiard ball colliding elastically: first ball stops, second ball moves at first ball's original speed
distractors: Total energy and speed are conserved
Total force and momentum are conserved

facts.correctAnswer: Some kinetic energy converts to thermal energy, sound, or deformation
acceptableAlternatives: KE lost to heat, deformation, sound in inelastic collisions
facts.quizQuestion: In an inelastic collision, where does the 'lost' kinetic energy go?
facts.explanation: In inelastic collisions, some KE converts to: thermal energy (internal energy from deformation), sound energy, or deformation energy (permanent shape changes). Total energy is still conserved — only the mechanical portion decreases. Car crash crumple zones intentionally maximize deformation to dissipate KE and protect passengers.
facts.visualDescription: Two clay balls colliding with heat and sound symbols indicating energy conversion from KE
distractors: It transfers to the environment as gravitational PE
It is stored in the spring between objects

facts.correctAnswer: The center of mass moves at constant velocity (if no external net force)
acceptableAlternatives: CM velocity is constant with no external force
v_cm = p_total / m_total = constant
facts.quizQuestion: When two objects collide (with no external net force), how does the center of mass of the system move?
facts.explanation: The center of mass continues to move at constant velocity v_cm = p_total/m_total throughout the collision, because the total momentum is conserved. Internal forces (the collision forces) do not change the CM velocity. Even in an explosion, the CM of the fragments continues moving at the pre-explosion velocity. This is a powerful constraint for analyzing collisions.
facts.visualDescription: Two colliding objects with the CM marked as a dot moving in a straight line at constant velocity through the collision

momentum is NOT conserved when external forces act
facts.quizQuestion: Under what condition is conservation of momentum NOT valid for a system?
facts.explanation: Conservation of momentum requires zero net external force. If ΣF_external ≠ 0, then Δp_total = ΣF_external × Δt ≠ 0. A common example: a ball bouncing off a wall has its momentum changed by the normal force from the wall (external force). During a short collision, external forces (gravity, normal) are often neglected because the collision forces are much larger — this is called the 'impulse approximation.'
facts.visualDescription: A system diagram with external forces labeled as changing the total momentum arrow

facts.correctAnswer: Angular displacement (Δθ)
acceptableAlternatives: Δθ
angle swept in radians
facts.quizQuestion: What rotational analog of displacement measures the angle through which an object rotates, typically in radians?
facts.explanation: Angular displacement Δθ = θ_f − θ_i, measured in radians (rad). One full rotation = 2π rad ≈ 6.28 rad. Like linear displacement, angular displacement is a vector (direction by right-hand rule for the axis). It is the rotational equivalent of Δx in linear kinematics.
facts.visualDescription: A rotating disk with initial and final positions marked and the swept angle Δθ labeled

facts.correctAnswer: Angular velocity (ω)
acceptableAlternatives: omega
ω = Δθ/Δt
facts.quizQuestion: What rotational analog of velocity measures the rate of change of angular displacement, in units of rad/s?
facts.explanation: Angular velocity ω = Δθ/Δt, in rad/s. It is the rotational equivalent of linear velocity. A positive ω means counterclockwise rotation (by convention). Related to period: ω = 2π/T and frequency: ω = 2πf. Connects to linear speed: v = rω.
facts.visualDescription: A rotating wheel with ω labeled as the rate of angular change in rad/s

facts.correctAnswer: Angular acceleration (α)
acceptableAlternatives: alpha
α = Δω/Δt
facts.quizQuestion: What rotational analog of acceleration measures the rate of change of angular velocity, in units of rad/s²?
facts.explanation: Angular acceleration α = Δω/Δt, in rad/s². It is the rotational equivalent of linear acceleration. If α and ω are in the same direction, the object speeds up rotationally; if opposite, it slows down. Connects to tangential acceleration: a_t = rα.
facts.visualDescription: A wheel with ω increasing over time, labeled with α = Δω/Δt in rad/s²

facts.correctAnswer: Rotational equations mirror linear equations (θ↔x, ω↔v, α↔a)
acceptableAlternatives: angular kinematic equations have same form as linear equations
facts.quizQuestion: How do the rotational kinematic equations for constant angular acceleration relate to the linear kinematic equations?
facts.explanation: The rotational equations mirror the linear ones: ω = ω₀ + αt; θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt²; ω² = ω₀² + 2αΔθ. Each linear variable has a rotational analog (x→θ, v→ω, a→α). If you know linear kinematics, you know rotational kinematics — just substitute the angular variables. The same logic and math applies.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side table showing linear and rotational kinematic equations with arrows connecting analogous quantities
distractors: Rotational equations only work for circles
The forms are similar but variables are inverted
Angular equations require mass in every term
Rotational kinematics doesn't apply to disks
Only ω = ω₀ + αt is analogous, rest differ
Rotational equations involve cross products always

facts.correctAnswer: v = rω
acceptableAlternatives: tangential speed equals radius times angular velocity
facts.quizQuestion: What equation connects the tangential (linear) speed v of a point on a rotating object to its angular velocity ω and distance r from the axis?
facts.explanation: v = rω. Points farther from the rotation axis (larger r) move faster tangentially even with the same ω. This explains why the rim of a wheel moves faster than a point near the hub. Derived from arc length s = rθ, differentiated with respect to time: v = ds/dt = r(dθ/dt) = rω.
facts.visualDescription: A rotating disk with two points at different radii, showing v₁ < v₂ for the same ω since r₁ < r₂

facts.correctAnswer: a_t = rα
acceptableAlternatives: tangential acceleration equals radius times angular acceleration
facts.quizQuestion: What equation relates the tangential linear acceleration of a point to the angular acceleration α and radius r?
facts.explanation: a_t = rα. This is the component of linear acceleration that is tangential to the circular path (causes speed change). The other component is centripetal: a_c = v²/r = rω². Total linear acceleration: a = √(a_t² + a_c²). For uniform circular motion: α = 0, so a_t = 0, leaving only centripetal acceleration.
facts.visualDescription: A rotating disk with a point on the rim showing tangential acceleration a_t = rα and centripetal acceleration a_c toward center

facts.correctAnswer: Torque (τ)
acceptableAlternatives: tau
twisting force
rotational analog of force
facts.quizQuestion: What quantity is the rotational analog of force, measuring the tendency of a force to cause rotation about an axis?
facts.explanation: Torque τ = rF sin θ, where r is the moment arm (distance from axis to force application point), F is the force, and θ is the angle between r and F. SI unit: N⋅m. Torque is what you apply when opening a door — same force applied farther from the hinge (larger r) produces greater torque. Also τ = r⊥F = rF⊥.
facts.visualDescription: A door with force applied at various distances from the hinge, showing torque increasing with distance

facts.correctAnswer: τ = rF sin θ
acceptableAlternatives: torque equals r times F times sine of angle
τ = r⊥F = rF⊥
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the torque τ produced by force F applied at distance r from the axis, at angle θ?
facts.explanation: τ = rF sin θ. Maximum torque when θ = 90° (force perpendicular to moment arm). Zero torque when θ = 0° or 180° (force along the moment arm — no tendency to rotate). Equivalently: τ = r⊥ × F (perpendicular distance from axis to the line of action of force) or τ = r × F⊥ (perpendicular component of force). SI unit: N⋅m.
facts.visualDescription: A wrench with force F at angle θ to the handle of length r, with torque calculation shown

facts.correctAnswer: Rotational inertia (moment of inertia, I)
acceptableAlternatives: moment of inertia
I = Σm_i r_i²
facts.quizQuestion: What quantity is the rotational analog of mass, measuring how hard it is to change an object's angular velocity?
facts.explanation: Rotational inertia (moment of inertia) I = Σm_i r_i² (sum of mass × distance² for all particles). Units: kg⋅m². More mass farther from the axis → larger I → harder to start/stop rotating. A solid disk has I = ½MR²; a thin ring has I = MR² (all mass at rim, so larger I). Figure skaters reduce I by pulling in their arms to spin faster.
facts.visualDescription: A figure skater pulling in arms (smaller I, faster spin) vs. arms extended (larger I, slower spin)

facts.correctAnswer: I = Σm_i r_i²
acceptableAlternatives: sum of mass times radius squared
moment of inertia = sum of m times r squared
facts.quizQuestion: What equation defines the moment of inertia I for a collection of point masses?
facts.explanation: I = Σm_i r_i² sums the contribution of each particle: mass × (distance from axis)². Units: kg⋅m². The same total mass distributed farther from the axis gives larger I. For continuous objects, this becomes an integral. I depends on the chosen axis — the same object can have different I for different rotation axes.
facts.visualDescription: Multiple masses at different distances from an axis, each labeled m_i and r_i, with I = Σm_i r_i² shown

facts.correctAnswer: Στ = Iα
acceptableAlternatives: net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration
facts.quizQuestion: What equation is the rotational analog of Newton's Second Law (F_net = ma)?
facts.explanation: Στ = Iα is Newton's Second Law for rotation. Net torque causes angular acceleration, proportional to I (rotational inertia). Greater torque → greater α; greater I → smaller α for same torque. Just as F = ma uses mass as the linear inertia, Στ = Iα uses I as the rotational inertia. Units: N⋅m = (kg⋅m²)(rad/s²).
facts.visualDescription: A rotating disk with applied torque τ and resulting angular acceleration α labeled, with Στ = Iα shown

facts.correctAnswer: Rotational equilibrium: Στ = 0
acceptableAlternatives: net torque equals zero
sum of torques is zero
facts.quizQuestion: What condition must be satisfied for an object to be in rotational equilibrium (no angular acceleration)?
facts.explanation: Rotational equilibrium requires Στ = 0 (net torque = 0). This means all clockwise torques equal all counterclockwise torques. An object in both translational (ΣF = 0) and rotational (Στ = 0) equilibrium is in static equilibrium. Seesaw problems, ladder problems, and hanging signs use this condition.
facts.visualDescription: A seesaw balanced with a heavy child close to the center and a light child far from the center

facts.correctAnswer: Moment arm (lever arm)
acceptableAlternatives: perpendicular distance from axis to line of action of force
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the perpendicular distance from the rotation axis to the line of action of the force, which determines torque magnitude?
facts.explanation: Moment arm (or lever arm) r⊥ is the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line along which the force acts. Torque τ = F × r⊥. This is why applying a force far from the pivot (long moment arm) creates more torque. A force aimed directly at the pivot has moment arm = 0 and creates no torque.
facts.visualDescription: A wrench with the perpendicular distance from the bolt to the force line labeled as the moment arm

facts.correctAnswer: The distribution of mass relative to the rotation axis
acceptableAlternatives: how far mass is from the axis
mass farther from axis → larger I
facts.quizQuestion: What factor, beyond total mass alone, determines an object's moment of inertia?
facts.explanation: I = Σm_i r_i² shows that the SAME total mass can produce vastly different I depending on how far that mass is from the rotation axis. A solid sphere (I = 2/5 MR²) and a hollow sphere (I = 2/3 MR²) of the same mass and radius have different I. Redistributing mass to larger radii dramatically increases rotational inertia.
facts.visualDescription: Two objects of equal mass — one compact (small I) and one spread out (large I) — both shown rotating
distractors: The speed of rotation determines I
Only the radius of the object determines I
Temperature of the object determines I

facts.correctAnswer: Newton-meter (N⋅m)
acceptableAlternatives: N·m
newton meter
facts.quizQuestion: What is the SI unit of torque?
facts.explanation: Torque unit = N⋅m (newton-meter). This is the same as the unit of work (joules), but they are different physical quantities — torque is a vector related to rotation, while work is a scalar energy. To avoid confusion, torque is written as N⋅m (not joules). Torque = rF sin θ: meters × newtons × dimensionless = N⋅m.
facts.visualDescription: A wrench applying 10 N at 0.3 m from the bolt: τ = 10 × 0.3 = 3 N⋅m

facts.correctAnswer: ΣF = 0 AND Στ = 0
acceptableAlternatives: both net force and net torque equal zero
facts.quizQuestion: What two conditions must BOTH be satisfied for an object to be in complete static equilibrium?
facts.explanation: Static equilibrium requires both: (1) ΣF = 0 (no translational acceleration — object not accelerating linearly) AND (2) Στ = 0 (no rotational acceleration — object not spinning). Meeting only one condition isn't enough: an object could have zero net force but still rotate (couple of forces), or zero net torque but still translate.
facts.visualDescription: A sign hanging from two ropes with force arrows showing ΣF = 0, and a balanced beam showing Στ = 0
distractors: All forces must be equal in magnitude

facts.correctAnswer: Counterclockwise torques are positive; clockwise are negative (by convention)
acceptableAlternatives: CCW = positive, CW = negative torque convention
facts.quizQuestion: In AP Physics 1 problems (2D), what sign convention is used for torque?
facts.explanation: Counterclockwise (CCW) torques are positive, clockwise (CW) torques are negative (by convention — it can be reversed if stated). For rotational equilibrium: Στ = 0 means Στ_CCW = Στ_CW. This mirrors the sign convention for angular displacement and velocity: CCW is the positive rotational direction (consistent with the right-hand rule pointing out of the page).
facts.visualDescription: A pivot point with CCW torque arrow labeled positive and CW torque arrow labeled negative
distractors: Sign depends on the direction of gravity
Sign depends on whether the object rotates
Upward forces create positive torque, downward create negative
Torque has no sign — it is always positive
The sign depends on the moment arm direction
Left-side forces are positive; right-side forces are negative
Torque sign depends on the mass of the object

facts.correctAnswer: m₁ × d₁ = m₂ × d₂ (balanced torques about the pivot)
acceptableAlternatives: force times distance from pivot must be equal on both sides
facts.quizQuestion: What equation must hold for a seesaw to be balanced, where m₁ and m₂ are masses at distances d₁ and d₂ from the pivot?
facts.explanation: m₁g × d₁ = m₂g × d₂ (g cancels) → m₁d₁ = m₂d₂. This is rotational equilibrium: torques about the pivot must sum to zero. A heavier child (larger m₂) must sit closer to the pivot (smaller d₂) to balance a lighter child farther out. This is the lever principle — Archimedes said 'Give me a long enough lever and a place to stand, and I will move the world.'
facts.visualDescription: A seesaw balanced with m₁ at distance d₁ on the left and m₂ at distance d₂ on the right, labeled m₁d₁ = m₂d₂

facts.correctAnswer: Linear ↔ Rotational: m↔I, v↔ω, a↔α, F↔τ, p↔L, x↔θ
acceptableAlternatives: rotational analogs: mass→I, velocity→ω, force→torque, momentum→L
facts.quizQuestion: What are the rotational analogs of mass, velocity, acceleration, force, and momentum?
facts.explanation: Every linear quantity has a rotational analog: mass m ↔ moment of inertia I; velocity v ↔ angular velocity ω; acceleration a ↔ angular acceleration α; force F ↔ torque τ; momentum p ↔ angular momentum L; displacement x ↔ angular displacement θ. The same equation forms hold: F=ma→τ=Iα, p=mv→L=Iω, J=FΔt→ΔL=τΔt.
facts.visualDescription: A two-column table showing linear quantities on the left and rotational analogs on the right
distractors: m↔I only; all others are different
There are no useful rotational analogs to linear quantities
The analogy breaks down when motion is circular
m↔I and v↔ω only; the other analogs don't hold
Angular momentum L has no linear analog

facts.correctAnswer: Solid disk: I = ½MR²; Thin ring: I = MR²
acceptableAlternatives: ring has larger moment of inertia than disk of same mass and radius
facts.quizQuestion: For equal mass M and radius R, which has a larger moment of inertia: a solid disk or a thin ring?
facts.explanation: Thin ring: I = MR² (all mass at radius R). Solid disk: I = ½MR² (mass distributed from 0 to R). The ring has I = 2× the disk's I because all ring mass is at the maximum radius. A hollow cylinder will roll down an incline slower than a solid cylinder of the same M and R because of its larger I and thus larger rotational KE fraction.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison of a solid disk (I = ½MR²) and a hollow ring (I = MR²) with equal M and R
distractors: Solid disk: I = MR²; Thin ring: I = ½MR²
Solid disk: I = ⅔MR²; Thin ring: I = MR²
Solid disk: I = ½MR²; Thin ring: I = 2MR²
The moment of inertia is equal for both shapes
Solid disk: I = ¼MR²; Thin ring: I = MR²
Solid sphere: I = ½MR²; ring I = MR² (confused with sphere)

facts.correctAnswer: Maximum torque when force is perpendicular to the moment arm (θ = 90°)
acceptableAlternatives: sin 90° = 1 gives maximum torque
facts.quizQuestion: At what angle between the force and the moment arm is torque maximized?
facts.explanation: τ = rF sin θ is maximized when sin θ = 1, i.e., θ = 90°. This is why door handles are positioned perpendicular to the door — and why pushing a door at the hinge (θ → 0°, sin θ → 0) barely opens it. Also: zero torque when force is along the moment arm (θ = 0° or 180°). A wrench is most effective when pulled perpendicular to its handle.
facts.visualDescription: A door with three force arrows at angles 0°, 45°, and 90°, showing torque magnitudes increasing with angle
distractors: When force is parallel to the moment arm (θ = 0°)
Torque is the same regardless of angle
When force points toward the pivot
When force equals the weight of the object

facts.correctAnswer: K_rot = ½Iω²
acceptableAlternatives: rotational kinetic energy equals half I omega squared
½Iω²
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the rotational kinetic energy of an object with moment of inertia I spinning at angular velocity ω?
facts.explanation: K_rot = ½Iω² is the rotational analog of K = ½mv². I plays the role of mass, ω plays the role of v. Units: (kg⋅m²)(rad/s)² = kg⋅m²/s² = J. For a rolling object, total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω² (translational + rotational). Objects with more mass at the rim (larger I) have more rotational KE for the same ω.
facts.visualDescription: A spinning flywheel with I and ω labeled, and K_rot = ½Iω² shown

facts.correctAnswer: K_total = ½mv² + ½Iω²
acceptableAlternatives: total KE of rolling object is translational plus rotational
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the total kinetic energy of an object rolling without slipping?
facts.explanation: K_total = ½mv² + ½Iω². Rolling objects have both translational KE (center of mass moving) and rotational KE (spinning about CM). For rolling without slipping: v_cm = Rω, so ω = v/R. Substituting: K = ½mv² + ½I(v/R)². A hollow cylinder (I = MR²) has more rotational KE than a solid cylinder (I = ½MR²) and rolls slower down a ramp.
facts.visualDescription: A rolling cylinder with both v (translational) and ω (rotational) arrows labeled, showing both KE contributions

facts.correctAnswer: v_cm = Rω (rolling without slipping condition)
acceptableAlternatives: linear speed equals radius times angular speed for rolling
facts.quizQuestion: What condition relates the center-of-mass velocity to the angular velocity for an object rolling without slipping?
facts.explanation: v_cm = Rω (rolling without slipping). This means the contact point has zero velocity relative to the ground — no skidding. If v_cm > Rω, the wheel is sliding forward (spinning tires on ice). If v_cm < Rω, the wheel is sliding backward (braking skid). This constraint connects linear and rotational motion for rolling problems.
facts.visualDescription: A wheel rolling without slipping with v_cm labeled at center and ω labeled for rotation, showing the contact point is momentarily at rest

facts.correctAnswer: W = τΔθ
acceptableAlternatives: work done by torque equals torque times angular displacement
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the work done by a constant torque τ through an angular displacement Δθ?
facts.explanation: W = τΔθ is the rotational analog of W = Fd. Work done by torque is positive when torque and angular displacement are in the same rotational direction. Units: (N⋅m)(rad) = N⋅m = J (radians are dimensionless). This connects to the work-energy theorem: W = ΔK_rot = Δ(½Iω²).
facts.visualDescription: A wheel rotated by applied torque τ through angle Δθ with work W = τΔθ labeled

facts.correctAnswer: L = Iω
acceptableAlternatives: angular momentum equals moment of inertia times angular velocity
facts.quizQuestion: What equation defines angular momentum L for a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis?
facts.explanation: L = Iω is the rotational analog of p = mv. Angular momentum has units of kg⋅m²/s. Like linear momentum, angular momentum is conserved when there is no external torque. For a point mass: L = mvr. The direction of L follows the right-hand rule — curl fingers in direction of rotation, thumb points in direction of L.
facts.visualDescription: A spinning wheel with I and ω labeled, and L = Iω shown with an axial arrow

facts.correctAnswer: ΔL = τΔt
acceptableAlternatives: angular impulse equals torque times time
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the angular impulse, analogous to the linear impulse J = FΔt?
facts.explanation: ΔL = τΔt is the rotational analog of J = FΔt = Δp. A torque applied over a time interval changes the angular momentum. This follows from Στ = Iα = I(Δω/Δt) → τΔt = IΔω = ΔL. The angular impulse equals the area under a τ-t graph, just as linear impulse equals area under an F-t graph.
facts.visualDescription: A torque-time graph with shaded area labeled ΔL = τΔt

facts.correctAnswer: Conservation of angular momentum: L_i = L_f when Στ_ext = 0
acceptableAlternatives: angular momentum conserved when no external torque
facts.quizQuestion: What law explains why a figure skater spins faster when pulling in their arms?
facts.explanation: Conservation of angular momentum: L = Iω = constant when Στ_external = 0. When the skater pulls in arms, I decreases (mass moves closer to axis) → ω must increase to keep L = Iω constant. This explains spinning divers tucking, pulsars spinning up, and gyroscopes maintaining orientation. L is always conserved for isolated systems.
facts.visualDescription: A figure skater going from arms-out (large I, small ω) to arms-in (small I, large ω), with L constant
distractors: Conservation of kinetic energy for rotation

facts.correctAnswer: Gravitational force provides centripetal force: Gm₁m₂/r² = mv²/r
acceptableAlternatives: gravity = centripetal force for orbiting satellites
facts.quizQuestion: For an orbiting satellite, what equation expresses the balance between gravitational force and the centripetal force requirement?
facts.explanation: GMm/r² = mv²/r → v = √(GM/r). Orbital speed depends on the central mass M and orbit radius r, NOT the satellite's mass m. Larger orbit radius → smaller orbital speed. Satellites in low orbit move faster than those in high orbit. This explains why geosynchronous satellites (24-hr period) must be at a specific altitude (~36,000 km).
facts.visualDescription: A satellite in circular orbit with gravity arrow pointing toward Earth equaling the centripetal force requirement

facts.correctAnswer: Kepler's Third Law: T² ∝ r³ (T²/r³ = constant)
acceptableAlternatives: period squared proportional to orbital radius cubed
facts.quizQuestion: What law states that the square of a planet's orbital period is proportional to the cube of its average orbital radius?
facts.explanation: Kepler's Third Law: T² = (4π²/GM) r³, or T²/r³ = 4π²/GM = constant for all planets orbiting the same star. If you double the orbital radius, the period increases by a factor of 2^(3/2) ≈ 2.83. This can be derived by combining GMm/r² = mv²/r with T = 2πr/v.
facts.visualDescription: Graph of T² vs. r³ for planets in the solar system, showing a straight line

facts.correctAnswer: Simple harmonic motion (SHM)
acceptableAlternatives: SHM
oscillation with restoring force proportional to displacement
facts.quizQuestion: What type of periodic motion occurs when the restoring force is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed toward it?
facts.explanation: SHM is defined by F = −kx (Hooke's Law type restoring force). The negative sign means force always opposes displacement. Examples: mass on spring, small-angle pendulum. In SHM, the acceleration is also proportional to displacement: a = −(k/m)x. This leads to sinusoidal position vs. time graphs.
facts.visualDescription: A mass-spring system oscillating back and forth with a sinusoidal position-time graph beside it

facts.correctAnswer: T = 2π√(m/k)
acceptableAlternatives: period of mass-spring equals 2 pi root m over k
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the period of oscillation for a mass-spring system in SHM?
facts.explanation: T = 2π√(m/k). Period increases with larger mass (harder to accelerate) and decreases with larger spring constant (stiffer spring, stronger restoring force). Critically: period is INDEPENDENT of amplitude. Doubling the mass increases T by √2. Quadrupling the spring constant halves T. No gravity term — works identically horizontally or vertically.
facts.visualDescription: Mass m on spring k oscillating with period T labeled, and the equation T = 2π√(m/k) shown

facts.correctAnswer: T = 2π√(L/g)
acceptableAlternatives: period of simple pendulum equals 2 pi root L over g
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the period of a simple pendulum (small-angle approximation) with length L?
facts.explanation: T = 2π√(L/g). Period depends on length L and gravitational acceleration g — NOT on mass or amplitude (for small angles < ~15°). Longer pendulum → longer period. This is why grandfather clocks have long pendulums. On the Moon (smaller g), the same pendulum has a longer period. This equation fails for large swing angles.
facts.visualDescription: A simple pendulum of length L swinging with period T, and the equation T = 2π√(L/g) shown

facts.correctAnswer: f = 1/T (frequency is the reciprocal of period)
acceptableAlternatives: T = 1/f
frequency equals one over period
facts.quizQuestion: What is the relationship between frequency (f) and period (T) in oscillatory motion?
facts.explanation: f = 1/T, or equivalently T = 1/f. Frequency measures how many cycles per second (Hz = cycles/s). Period measures how long one cycle takes (seconds). A pendulum with T = 2 s has f = 0.5 Hz. A vibrating string at 440 Hz (musical A) has T = 1/440 ≈ 0.00227 s. Angular frequency ω = 2πf = 2π/T.
facts.visualDescription: A wave with one complete cycle labeled with period T, and the equation f = 1/T shown

facts.correctAnswer: Amplitude (A)
acceptableAlternatives: maximum displacement from equilibrium
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the maximum displacement from the equilibrium position in simple harmonic motion?
facts.explanation: Amplitude A is the maximum displacement in either direction from the equilibrium (rest) position. For a mass-spring system, if you pull the mass 0.1 m from equilibrium and release it, A = 0.1 m. The system oscillates between +A and −A. Amplitude does NOT affect the period (T = 2π√(m/k) — no A term). Energy ∝ A².
facts.visualDescription: A sinusoidal wave with the maximum height labeled as amplitude A

facts.correctAnswer: At the equilibrium position (x = 0)
acceptableAlternatives: maximum speed at equilibrium
center of oscillation
facts.quizQuestion: In simple harmonic motion, at what position does the oscillating object have maximum speed?
facts.explanation: Maximum speed occurs at x = 0 (equilibrium), where all the energy is kinetic. At maximum displacement (x = ±A), all energy is potential (U = ½kA²) and speed = 0. Energy conservation: ½mv²_max = ½kA², so v_max = A√(k/m) = Aω. This can be confirmed from the cosine position equation: dx/dt = −Aω sin(ωt), maximum when sin = ±1.
facts.visualDescription: SHM path showing zero speed at extreme positions and maximum speed at center
distractors: At the midpoint between equilibrium and max displacement
At any position — speed is constant in SHM
At the same position as maximum acceleration

facts.correctAnswer: At maximum displacement (x = ±A)
acceptableAlternatives: at the endpoints of oscillation
at x = A or x = -A
facts.quizQuestion: In simple harmonic motion, where does the oscillating object have maximum acceleration?
facts.explanation: Maximum acceleration occurs at x = ±A (maximum displacement) because F = −kx is largest there: a = −(k/m)x, maximum when x = ±A. At equilibrium (x = 0), acceleration = 0. Acceleration and position are 180° out of phase (a = −ω²x). Acceleration always points toward equilibrium (restoring). Speed is zero where acceleration is maximum.
facts.visualDescription: SHM with largest force arrows at the endpoints (maximum displacement) pointing back toward center
distractors: At the midpoint between equilibrium and maximum
Halfway between equilibrium and maximum displacement
At both x = 0 and x = A equally

facts.correctAnswer: E_total = ½kA² = constant
acceptableAlternatives: total energy in SHM equals half k A squared
energy proportional to A squared
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the total mechanical energy of a mass-spring simple harmonic oscillator in terms of amplitude A?
facts.explanation: E_total = ½kA². At maximum displacement (x = A): E = ½kA² (all PE). At equilibrium (x = 0): E = ½mv²_max = ½kA² (all KE). At any point: ½kx² + ½mv² = ½kA². Since period T = 2π√(m/k) is independent of A, the energy and period are independent — greater amplitude means more energy but same period.
facts.visualDescription: Bar chart showing E = ½kA² constant, split between KE and PE at different positions

facts.correctAnswer: Period is independent of amplitude in SHM
acceptableAlternatives: T does not depend on amplitude
same period regardless of how far you pull the mass
facts.quizQuestion: How does the amplitude of oscillation affect the period of a simple harmonic oscillator?
facts.explanation: Period T = 2π√(m/k) contains no amplitude term. This is the key property of SHM — called isochrony (Galileo discovered this for pendulums). A clock pendulum keeps accurate time regardless of how far it swings (within small-angle approximation). Larger amplitude means more energy but the system takes the exact same time per cycle.
facts.visualDescription: Two springs with masses oscillating at different amplitudes but with equal period timing arrows
distractors: Period is proportional to amplitude squared
Period increases with amplitude for springs but not pendulums
Period decreases with amplitude in all SHM
Period depends on amplitude for pendulums only
Amplitude must be small for period to be constant

facts.correctAnswer: Hertz (Hz) = cycles per second = s⁻¹
acceptableAlternatives: Hz = 1/s
frequency in cycles per second
facts.quizQuestion: What is the SI unit of frequency, and how is it defined?
facts.explanation: 1 Hz = 1 cycle/second = 1 s⁻¹ (inverse seconds). Named after Heinrich Hertz, who first demonstrated radio waves. A 60 Hz AC electrical system completes 60 cycles per second. Middle C on a piano vibrates at 261.6 Hz. Ultrasound medical imaging uses ~1-18 MHz (millions of Hz).
facts.visualDescription: A signal wave completing one cycle per second labeled as 1 Hz

facts.correctAnswer: Density (ρ = m/V)
acceptableAlternatives: rho = mass/volume
mass per unit volume
facts.quizQuestion: What property of matter equals mass divided by volume, with SI unit kg/m³?
facts.explanation: Density ρ = m/V. SI unit: kg/m³. Water has ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³; air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³; iron ≈ 7900 kg/m³. Objects denser than the surrounding fluid sink; less dense objects float. Density is an intrinsic property — a small piece and a large piece of the same material have identical density.
facts.visualDescription: A cube of material with mass m and volume V labeled, showing ρ = m/V

facts.correctAnswer: Pressure (P = F/A)
acceptableAlternatives: force per unit area
P = F⊥/A
facts.quizQuestion: What quantity equals the perpendicular force exerted per unit area on a surface, measured in pascals (Pa)?
facts.explanation: Pressure P = F/A. Only the component of force perpendicular to the surface counts. SI unit: 1 Pa = 1 N/m². Atmospheric pressure ≈ 101,325 Pa ≈ 101.3 kPa. Pressure acts equally in all directions in a static fluid. A sharp needle concentrates force on a tiny area → very high pressure → penetrates skin.
facts.visualDescription: A force F applied over area A, with P = F/A labeled, and a comparison of wide vs. narrow tip showing pressure difference

facts.correctAnswer: P = P₀ + ρgh
acceptableAlternatives: pressure increases with depth as rho g h
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the pressure at depth h in a static fluid with surface pressure P₀?
facts.explanation: P = P₀ + ρgh. Pressure increases linearly with depth. At depth h: the column of fluid above exerts pressure ρgh in addition to the surface pressure P₀. In the ocean, every 10 m of depth adds approximately 1 atm (≈ 100 kPa) of pressure (seawater ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³, g ≈ 10 m/s²: ρgh ≈ 1025 × 10 × 10 ≈ 100,000 Pa).
facts.visualDescription: A tank of fluid with depth h labeled, showing pressure P₀ at the surface and P = P₀ + ρgh at the bottom

facts.correctAnswer: Pascal's Principle
acceptableAlternatives: Pascal's law
pressure in enclosed fluid transmits equally in all directions
facts.quizQuestion: What principle states that a change in pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to every point in the fluid?
facts.explanation: Pascal's Principle: a pressure change ΔP applied at any point in a confined fluid transmits fully (undiminished) to all other parts. This is the basis of hydraulic systems: applying small force over small area creates pressure that multiplies force on a large-area piston (F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂). Used in car brake systems, hydraulic jacks, and dentist chairs.
facts.visualDescription: A hydraulic jack with small piston (small area, small force) connected to large piston (large area, large force)

facts.correctAnswer: F_b = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g
acceptableAlternatives: buoyant force equals rho times V times g
Archimedes' buoyancy formula
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the buoyant force on an object submerged in or floating on a fluid?
facts.explanation: F_b = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g (Archimedes' Principle). Buoyancy equals the weight of the fluid displaced. An object floats when F_b ≥ F_g, which means ρ_object ≤ ρ_fluid. A submerged object: buoyant force = ρ_fluid × V_object × g. For a floating object: ρ_fluid × V_displaced = ρ_object × V_object (the submerged fraction = ρ_object/ρ_fluid).
facts.visualDescription: An iceberg with a small portion above water and a large portion below, labeled with buoyancy and weight forces

facts.correctAnswer: Archimedes' Principle
acceptableAlternatives: the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid
facts.quizQuestion: What principle states that the buoyant force on a submerged object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces?
facts.explanation: Archimedes' Principle: F_b = weight of displaced fluid = ρ_fluid V_displaced g. This explains flotation, submarine depth control, and the apparent weight loss of objects in water. According to legend, Archimedes discovered this while stepping into a bathtub and shouted 'Eureka!' Ships float because their large hollow hull displaces enough water to support their weight.
facts.visualDescription: Archimedes overflowing a bathtub with text 'Eureka!' and the equation F_b = ρ_fluid V g

facts.correctAnswer: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (continuity equation)
acceptableAlternatives: flow rate is constant: A times v is constant
area times velocity is constant
facts.quizQuestion: What equation expresses conservation of mass for an incompressible fluid flowing through a pipe?
facts.explanation: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂: the volume flow rate Q = Av is constant. If a pipe narrows (A₂ < A₁), the fluid speeds up (v₂ > v₁) to maintain the same flow rate. This explains why water from a garden hose speeds up when you partly cover the nozzle. The continuity equation assumes incompressible, steady flow.
facts.visualDescription: A pipe that narrows from area A₁ to A₂, with fluid moving faster in the narrower section (v₂ > v₁)

facts.correctAnswer: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant
acceptableAlternatives: Bernoulli's equation
pressure plus dynamic pressure plus hydrostatic pressure is constant
facts.quizQuestion: What equation expresses conservation of energy for an ideal fluid flowing along a streamline?
facts.explanation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant (Bernoulli's equation). This is conservation of energy per unit volume: pressure energy + kinetic energy density + gravitational PE density. Faster fluid has lower pressure (½ρv² increases, P must decrease). This explains airplane lift (faster air over wing → lower pressure on top → net upward force) and the Venturi effect.
facts.visualDescription: A cross-section of an airplane wing with faster airflow on top (lower P) and slower below (higher P) creating lift

facts.correctAnswer: Bernoulli's Principle
acceptableAlternatives: faster fluid has lower pressure
facts.quizQuestion: What principle states that for a flowing fluid, regions of higher velocity have lower pressure?
facts.explanation: Bernoulli's Principle: in a steady flow, faster-moving fluid has lower pressure (from P + ½ρv² = constant when h is constant). Applications: airplane lift, Venturi meter (measuring flow speed), atomizers/sprayers, and the curveball in baseball (Magnus effect). Named after Daniel Bernoulli (1700–1782).
facts.visualDescription: A garden hose nozzle with high-speed flow and low-pressure region labeled

facts.correctAnswer: Pascal (Pa) = N/m² = kg/(m⋅s²)
acceptableAlternatives: 1 Pa = 1 N/m²
newton per square meter
facts.quizQuestion: What is the SI unit of pressure, and how is it expressed in base units?
facts.explanation: 1 Pa (pascal) = 1 N/m² = 1 kg/(m⋅s²). Named after Blaise Pascal. Atmospheric pressure ≈ 101,325 Pa ≈ 101.3 kPa. A gentle breeze creates about 1-2 Pa. A car tire is inflated to about 200,000 Pa (200 kPa) gauge pressure. Blood pressure is measured in mmHg (1 mmHg ≈ 133 Pa).
facts.visualDescription: Pressure gauge showing units of Pa = N/m² with atmospheric pressure labeled as ~101 kPa

facts.correctAnswer: Object floats when its average density ≤ fluid density
acceptableAlternatives: ρ_object ≤ ρ_fluid for floating
facts.quizQuestion: What determines whether an object floats or sinks in a fluid?
facts.explanation: An object floats if its average density ρ_object ≤ ρ_fluid; it sinks if ρ_object > ρ_fluid. Average density matters — a steel ship floats because the hull encloses air, making the ship's average density less than water. Ice floats on water because ρ_ice (917 kg/m³) < ρ_water (1000 kg/m³). Quantitatively: submerged fraction = ρ_object/ρ_fluid.
facts.visualDescription: Three objects: one sinking (dense), one floating partially submerged (same density as fraction submerged), one on surface (less dense)

facts.correctAnswer: Gauge pressure = absolute pressure − atmospheric pressure
acceptableAlternatives: P_gauge = P_abs − P_atm
facts.quizQuestion: What is gauge pressure, and how does it differ from absolute pressure?
facts.explanation: Gauge pressure = P_absolute − P_atm. Most everyday pressure measurements (tire pressure, blood pressure) are gauge pressures — they measure pressure ABOVE atmospheric. A tire gauge reads 0 when deflated (at atmospheric pressure). For physics problems, use absolute pressure when calculating hydrostatic pressure or applying Bernoulli's equation.
facts.visualDescription: A tire pressure gauge showing 32 psi gauge, with atmospheric pressure (14.7 psi) labeled as the baseline

facts.quizQuestion: How does Newton's Third Law explain why a submerged object experiences a buoyant force?
facts.explanation: The object pushes down on the surrounding fluid (by its weight), and by Newton's Third Law, the fluid pushes back on the object upward. This upward reaction force is the buoyant force. Equivalently, the fluid pressure acts on all surfaces of the object — net upward pressure force because P increases with depth (bottom face has higher pressure than top face).
facts.visualDescription: A submerged block with pressure arrows on all surfaces, net upward force labeled as buoyancy from Newton's Third Law

facts.correctAnswer: Volume flow rate Q = Av

facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the volume flow rate Q (volume per unit time) through a pipe in terms of cross-sectional area A and fluid speed v?
facts.explanation: Q = Av, where Q has units of m³/s. The continuity equation says Q = A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ = constant for incompressible steady flow. A large pipe (large A) with slow flow can carry the same volume per second as a narrow pipe (small A) with fast flow. Flow rate is conserved (like current in a circuit — charge conservation analog).
facts.visualDescription: A pipe of area A with fluid moving at speed v, labeled Q = Av = constant throughout the pipe

facts.correctAnswer: Coefficient of restitution (e): e=1 elastic, e=0 perfectly inelastic
acceptableAlternatives: e = 1 for elastic, 0 for perfectly inelastic
facts.quizQuestion: What value of the coefficient of restitution (e = relative speed after/before collision) characterizes an elastic vs. perfectly inelastic collision?
facts.explanation: Coefficient of restitution e = (relative speed of separation)/(relative speed of approach). e = 1: elastic collision (no KE lost). e = 0: perfectly inelastic (objects stick, maximum KE loss). 0 < e < 1: partially inelastic. A rubber ball bouncing might have e ≈ 0.7–0.9; a clay ball e ≈ 0. On AP Physics 1, you classify by momentum/KE conservation rather than 'e' directly.
facts.visualDescription: A ball bouncing with rebound height showing e² fraction of original height
distractors: e depends only on mass ratio

facts.correctAnswer: Radian (rad)
acceptableAlternatives: SI unit for angular measurement
rad = arc length / radius
facts.quizQuestion: What is the SI unit for angular displacement and angular velocity, defined as arc length divided by radius?
facts.explanation: A radian is defined as θ = s/r (arc length/radius). One complete revolution = 2π rad ≈ 6.28 rad. 180° = π rad. 90° = π/2 rad. Radians are dimensionless (m/m), which is why angular velocity ω = Δθ/Δt is in rad/s and angular frequency ω = 2πf. Converting: degrees × (π/180°) = radians.
facts.visualDescription: A circle with arc length s equal to radius r labeled, showing an angle of 1 radian

facts.correctAnswer: Gravitational force
acceptableAlternatives: weight
F_g
force of gravity
facts.quizQuestion: What long-range attractive force acts between any two objects with mass, acting toward their centers?
facts.explanation: The gravitational force (F_g = mg near Earth's surface; F_g = Gm₁m₂/r² universally) is always attractive — it always pulls masses toward each other. It is the weakest of the four fundamental forces but has infinite range. Near Earth's surface, it acts straight downward with magnitude mg. On a free-body diagram, weight is always drawn from the object's center pointing downward.
facts.visualDescription: Earth pulling a ball toward its center with a downward force arrow labeled F_g = mg

facts.correctAnswer: Spring (elastic) force
acceptableAlternatives: elastic restoring force
F_spring
facts.quizQuestion: What contact force is exerted by a compressed or stretched elastic object, always directed toward the equilibrium position?
facts.explanation: The spring force (F_s = −kx) is a contact force exerted by any elastic material — springs, rubber bands, bungee cords, or even the floor (which flexes microscopically). It is a restoring force: always directed toward equilibrium, opposing displacement. Springs store energy as elastic PE (U_s = ½kx²) which can be released as kinetic energy.
facts.visualDescription: A spring connected to a wall and a mass, with the spring force arrow pointing toward the equilibrium position

facts.correctAnswer: Buoyant force
acceptableAlternatives: F_b
upward force from fluid on submerged object
facts.quizQuestion: What upward contact force does a fluid exert on a submerged or floating object, equal to the weight of displaced fluid?
facts.explanation: The buoyant force (F_b = ρ_fluid V_displaced g) acts upward on any object in a fluid. It arises because fluid pressure increases with depth — the bottom of a submerged object experiences greater pressure than the top, producing a net upward force. If F_b ≥ F_g, the object floats; if F_b < F_g, it sinks. On an FBD, buoyancy is drawn upward from the object's center.
facts.visualDescription: A submerged block with a large upward buoyant force arrow and a downward weight arrow, showing the net force determining whether it sinks or floats

acceptableAlternatives: 1 atm = 101.3 kPa
approximately 100,000 Pa
facts.quizQuestion: What is standard atmospheric pressure at sea level in pascals?
facts.explanation: Standard atmospheric pressure = 101,325 Pa ≈ 101.3 kPa ≈ 1 atm ≈ 14.7 psi. This is the weight of the entire air column above 1 m² of ground. It can support a column of mercury 760 mm tall (hence mmHg as a pressure unit). The pressure decreases with altitude because there is less air above.
facts.visualDescription: A barometer showing 760 mm of mercury, equivalent to 101.3 kPa atmospheric pressure

acceptableAlternatives: 1000 kg/m³
1 g/cm³
facts.quizQuestion: What is the density of fresh water at room temperature?
facts.explanation: Water density ρ = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³. This is the reference for specific gravity (ratio of object density to water density). Ice is less dense at ~917 kg/m³ (so it floats). Seawater is ~1025 kg/m³. Ethanol is ~789 kg/m³. Knowing water density lets you quickly compute buoyancy: F_b = 1000 × V × 10 N/m³ for objects in water.
facts.visualDescription: A 1 m³ cube of water with mass 1000 kg labeled, showing ρ = 1000 kg/m³

facts.correctAnswer: ½kx² joules (elastic PE stored)
acceptableAlternatives: U = ½kx² in joules
facts.quizQuestion: A spring with k = 200 N/m is compressed 0.1 m. What elastic potential energy is stored?
facts.explanation: U_s = ½kx² = ½ × 200 × (0.1)² = ½ × 200 × 0.01 = 1 J. This energy can be converted to kinetic energy when the spring releases. Notice that doubling the compression to 0.2 m gives U = ½ × 200 × 0.04 = 4 J — four times more energy for double the compression (energy scales as x²).
facts.visualDescription: Spring with k=200 N/m compressed 0.1 m, showing calculation of U = 1 J

facts.correctAnswer: Tip-to-tail method
acceptableAlternatives: head-to-tail method
graphical addition
facts.quizQuestion: What graphical method for adding vectors involves placing the tail of the second vector at the tip of the first?
facts.explanation: In the tip-to-tail (head-to-tail) method, you draw the first vector, then draw the second starting where the first ended. The resultant runs from the tail of the first to the tip of the last. This works for any number of vectors and is the foundation of graphical vector addition.

facts.correctAnswer: Adding the negative of the second vector
acceptableAlternatives: adding the opposite vector
facts.quizQuestion: Vector subtraction A − B is equivalent to what operation?
facts.explanation: Subtracting vector B from A is the same as adding the vector −B (same magnitude, opposite direction) to A. So A − B = A + (−B). This means you flip B's arrow 180° and then use tip-to-tail addition. This concept appears frequently in relative velocity and momentum change problems.
distractors: Finding the dot product of A and B

facts.correctAnswer: Add x-components together and y-components together separately
acceptableAlternatives: sum x-components and sum y-components separately
facts.quizQuestion: When adding two vectors using components, what is the correct procedure?
facts.explanation: To add vectors analytically: break each into x and y components using trig, add all x-components to get Rx, add all y-components to get Ry, then find the resultant magnitude R = √(Rx² + Ry²) and direction θ = arctan(Ry/Rx). This method is exact and preferred over graphical methods for precision.
distractors: Add all components into a single sum

facts.correctAnswer: When the vertical velocity component equals zero
acceptableAlternatives: at the peak, vy = 0
when vy = 0
facts.quizQuestion: At what point does a projectile reach its maximum height?
facts.explanation: Maximum height occurs when the vertical velocity component (vy) equals zero — the projectile momentarily stops moving upward before beginning to fall. At this instant the horizontal velocity vx is unchanged (still equals the initial horizontal velocity). Maximum height can be found using vy² = vy₀² − 2gΔy, setting vy = 0.

facts.quizQuestion: At what launch angle does a projectile achieve maximum horizontal range (assuming flat ground and same launch and landing height)?
facts.explanation: The horizontal range formula R = v₀²sin(2θ)/g is maximized when sin(2θ) = 1, which occurs at 2θ = 90°, giving θ = 45°. Note: angles that are supplements give the same range (e.g., 30° and 60° yield identical ranges). This 45° rule assumes level ground — on uneven terrain the optimal angle shifts.

facts.correctAnswer: The launch angle above horizontal equals the impact angle below horizontal
acceptableAlternatives: symmetric trajectory
same angle at landing as at launch
facts.quizQuestion: For a projectile launched and landing at the same height, what symmetry exists in the trajectory?
facts.explanation: A projectile's trajectory is symmetric about the peak. The time to rise equals the time to fall. The speed at any height on the way up equals the speed at the same height on the way down. The launch angle equals the impact angle, and the horizontal velocity is constant throughout. This symmetry simplifies many AP exam calculations.

acceptableAlternatives: they are independent directions

facts.quizQuestion: What common misconception about velocity and acceleration does AP Physics 1 specifically address?
facts.explanation: Velocity and acceleration can point in completely different directions. A ball thrown upward has upward velocity but downward acceleration (gravity). A car slowing down has forward velocity and backward acceleration. If acceleration is opposite to velocity, the object slows. If acceleration is perpendicular to velocity, it changes direction without changing speed (circular motion).

negative acceleration does not always mean slowing down
facts.quizQuestion: Why is 'deceleration' not the same as 'negative acceleration' in physics?
facts.explanation: Deceleration means an object is slowing down — its speed is decreasing. This happens when acceleration is opposite to velocity. A car moving in the negative direction that speeds up has a negative acceleration but is NOT decelerating. True deceleration depends on the relationship between velocity and acceleration directions, not the algebraic sign of acceleration alone.

facts.correctAnswer: The displacement during that time interval
acceptableAlternatives: displacement
change in position
facts.quizQuestion: On a velocity-time graph, what does the area under the curve between two times represent?
facts.explanation: The area under a v-t graph equals displacement: Δx = ∫v dt. For a rectangle (constant velocity), area = v × Δt = displacement. For a triangle (uniform acceleration from rest), area = ½ × base × height = ½vΔt. Areas below the time axis represent negative displacement (motion in the negative direction). This is one of the most-tested graph relationships in AP Physics 1.

facts.correctAnswer: Position is location from origin; displacement is change in position
acceptableAlternatives: position is x, displacement is Δx
facts.quizQuestion: What is the key distinction between position and displacement?
facts.explanation: Position (x) is an object's location relative to a chosen reference point (origin). Displacement (Δx) is the change in position: Δx = x_f − x_i. A car at position x = 10 m that moves to x = 6 m has displacement Δx = −4 m. Position depends on where you put the origin; displacement does not — it is the same regardless of origin choice.

acceptableAlternatives: distance is path length, displacement is net change in position
facts.quizQuestion: What is the key difference between distance and displacement?
facts.explanation: Distance is the total path length traveled — a scalar that is always positive. Displacement is the straight-line change in position — a vector that can be positive, negative, or zero. Running a full lap (400 m) gives distance = 400 m but displacement = 0 m. This distinction is crucial for correctly applying kinematic equations.

facts.quizQuestion: If object A moves at velocity vA and object B moves at velocity vB (both relative to ground), what is the velocity of A as seen by B?
facts.explanation: The velocity of A as observed from B is v_A/B = v_A − v_B. For example, if a train moves at +50 m/s and a car moves at +20 m/s, the train's velocity relative to the car is +30 m/s. If both move in the same direction at the same speed, the relative velocity is zero. This applies to any reference frame transformation.

facts.correctAnswer: R = v₀²sin(2θ)/g
acceptableAlternatives: R = (v0^2 * sin(2*theta)) / g
facts.quizQuestion: What equation gives the horizontal range R of a projectile launched at speed v₀ and angle θ on level ground?
facts.explanation: R = v₀²sin(2θ)/g combines the horizontal motion (x = v₀cosθ · t) and vertical motion (solved for total time of flight) into a single formula. Note that sin(2θ) = 2sinθcosθ, so this formula is equivalent to R = 2v₀²sinθcosθ/g. Maximum range at θ = 45° because sin(90°) = 1.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the speed of a projectile at the peak of its trajectory?
facts.explanation: At the peak, vy = 0, so the only velocity is the horizontal component vx = v₀cosθ. This is the minimum speed the projectile has during flight (for a launch angle between 0° and 90°). A common mistake is to say speed is zero at the peak — it is only zero if launched straight up (θ = 90°). For any other angle, the projectile still has horizontal velocity at the top.

facts.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity is 9.8 m/s² downward at ALL points along the trajectory — including the peak. A common misconception is that acceleration is zero at the peak because velocity is momentarily zero vertically. But gravity never 'turns off.' The acceleration is constant throughout the entire flight for any projectile (ignoring air resistance).

facts.correctAnswer: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx
acceptableAlternatives: vf^2 = vi^2 + 2a*delta_x
facts.quizQuestion: Which kinematic equation is most useful when time is unknown and you need to relate velocity, acceleration, and displacement?
facts.explanation: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx is the kinematic equation that contains no time variable. It directly connects final velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and displacement. Use this when time is neither given nor asked for — for example, finding the speed of a ball after rolling down a ramp of known length with known acceleration.

facts.correctAnswer: Internal forces act between objects within the system; external forces act from outside
acceptableAlternatives: internal forces are within the system, external forces are from outside the system
facts.quizQuestion: In systems thinking, what is the difference between internal and external forces?
facts.explanation: When you define a system boundary, forces between objects INSIDE that boundary are internal forces — they cancel in pairs by Newton's 3rd law and do not change the system's total momentum. Forces from OUTSIDE the boundary are external forces — these DO change the system's total momentum. Choosing the right system boundary simplifies many force problems dramatically.

facts.quizQuestion: Where is the center of mass located in an object with uniform density and symmetric shape?
facts.explanation: For uniform density objects with geometric symmetry (sphere, uniform rod, cylinder, etc.), the center of mass coincides with the geometric center. For example, the CM of a uniform meter stick is at the 50 cm mark. For non-uniform objects or asymmetric shapes, the CM is pulled toward the heavier region. This symmetry argument is often used on AP free-response problems.

facts.quizQuestion: What formula gives the x-coordinate of the center of mass for a system of particles?
facts.explanation: The center of mass position is the mass-weighted average position: x_cm = Σ(mᵢxᵢ)/M where M is total mass. For two equal masses, x_cm is exactly halfway between them. For unequal masses, x_cm is closer to the heavier mass. This formula extends to 2D (also compute y_cm separately). facts.explanation: A free-body diagram shows ALL forces acting ON the chosen object — gravity, normal force, friction, tension, applied forces, etc. You should NEVER include forces that the object exerts on others (Newton's 3rd law reaction forces go on a separate FBD for that other object). Including the wrong forces is one of the most common AP exam errors.

facts.explanation: N = mg only when: (1) the surface is horizontal, and (2) the object has no vertical acceleration. On an incline, N = mgcosθ (less than weight).
