facts.explanation: The truck exerts exactly the same magnitude force on the bicycle as the bicycle exerts on the truck — Newton's 3rd law guarantees this, regardless of mass or speed. The difference in damage/acceleration is because F = ma: the bicycle has much smaller mass, so the same force produces far greater acceleration. facts.explanation: By Newton's 2nd law (F_net = ma), acceleration is always in the same direction as the net force. facts.explanation: The universal law of gravitation F = Gm₁m₂/r² shows that force is proportional to 1/r². Doubling r means r² increases by 4, so F decreases to F/4 (one-fourth). facts.explanation: The coefficient of friction μ = f/N is a dimensionless ratio that characterizes how 'grippy' two surfaces are. Ice on ice: μ ≈ 0.03. Rubber on dry pavement: μ ≈ 0.7. facts.explanation: At a PE minimum: if displaced slightly, the restoring force pushes back toward the minimum → stable equilibrium (like a ball at the bottom of a bowl). At a PE maximum: if displaced slightly, the force pushes AWAY from the maximum → unstable equilibrium (like a ball balanced on top of a hill). A flat PE region means zero force — neutral equilibrium. The force at any point equals the negative slope of the PE curve: F = −dU/dx.

facts.quizQuestion: What does an energy bar chart (LOL diagram) show in AP Physics 1?
facts.explanation: Energy bar charts (sometimes called LOL diagrams) show the distribution of energy at two moments in time. The height of each bar represents an energy quantity (KE, gravitational PE, elastic PE, thermal energy from friction). Total bar heights on left equal total on right — energy is conserved. These diagrams make energy tracking visual: a decreasing KE bar paired with an increasing PE bar (e.g., a ball rolling uphill) shows conversion between forms.

facts.correctAnswer: Power is the rate of energy transfer: P = W/t = Fv (in Watts = J/s)

facts.quizQuestion: What does power measure, and how is it related to force and velocity?
facts.explanation: Power P = W/Δt = ΔE/Δt is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. For a constant force F moving an object at constant speed v, P = F·v (in Watts = J/s). A 100 W light bulb uses 100 J every second. A car engine rated at 150 kW can deliver 150,000 J per second. P = Fv is especially useful when force and velocity are both known.

acceptableAlternatives: η = W_useful / W_input × 100%
efficiency = useful output / total input
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for efficiency of an energy conversion process?
facts.explanation: Efficiency η = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%. A machine that converts 80 J of the 100 J input into useful work has efficiency = 80%. The remaining 20 J goes to thermal energy (waste heat). Efficiency is always between 0% and 100% — conservation of energy prevents output exceeding input. The most efficient real machines convert about 40-60% of input to useful work.

facts.correctAnswer: W_spring = ½kx² (energy stored equals work done to compress or stretch)
acceptableAlternatives: elastic PE = ½kx²

facts.quizQuestion: How much work is done to compress or stretch a spring by displacement x from its natural length?
facts.explanation: Because the spring force varies (F = kx), we cannot use W = Fd directly. The work done is the area under the F-vs-x graph (a triangle): W = ½kx². This equals the elastic potential energy stored: U_e = ½kx². Doubling the stretch quadruples the stored energy. At x = 0 (equilibrium), no energy is stored. This is one of the most-used energy facts in AP Physics 1.

facts.correctAnswer: Doubling speed quadruples kinetic energy (KE ∝ v²)
acceptableAlternatives: KE is proportional to v squared; double v means 4× KE
facts.quizQuestion: If a car's speed doubles, by what factor does its kinetic energy change?
facts.explanation: KE = ½mv². If v doubles (2v), KE = ½m(2v)² = ½m·4v² = 4 × (½mv²) = 4 times the original KE. Tripling speed gives 9× KE. This nonlinear relationship is why highway driving is far more dangerous than city driving — stopping from 60 mph requires 4× the stopping distance compared to 30 mph. The work-energy theorem shows this extra KE requires 4× the work to remove.
distractors: Kinetic energy remains the same if mass is constant
Kinetic energy is proportional to speed, not speed squared

facts.correctAnswer: The net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy
acceptableAlternatives: W_net = ΔKE = KE_f − KE_i
facts.quizQuestion: What does the Work-Energy Theorem state about the relationship between work and kinetic energy?
facts.explanation: The Work-Energy Theorem states: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv_f² − ½mv_i². The net work (sum of work done by ALL forces) equals the change in kinetic energy. Positive net work increases KE (object speeds up). Negative net work decreases KE (object slows down). Zero net work means constant KE (constant speed). This theorem connects force and motion through energy.
distractors: Net work equals total potential energy

facts.correctAnswer: v = √(2gh) for an object falling from height h from rest (ignoring friction)
acceptableAlternatives: speed at bottom = sqrt(2gh); from mgh = ½mv²
facts.quizQuestion: Using conservation of energy, what is the speed of an object that falls from height h starting from rest?
facts.explanation: Setting initial PE = final KE: mgh = ½mv² → v = √(2gh). Note that mass cancels — all objects fall to the same speed from the same height in the absence of friction (regardless of mass). For h = 5 m: v = √(2 × 9.8 × 5) = √98 ≈ 9.9 m/s. This is one of the most useful conservation of energy shortcuts for AP problems.

facts.correctAnswer: A 746 W motor equals 1 horsepower; human body averages ~100 W at rest (metabolic rate)
acceptableAlternatives: human resting metabolic power ≈ 100 W
facts.quizQuestion: In real-world terms, approximately how many Watts does 1 horsepower equal?
facts.explanation: 1 horsepower (hp) = 746 W ≈ 750 W. Car engines are rated in horsepower (e.g., 200 hp ≈ 149 kW). Human resting metabolic rate ≈ 80–100 W. A cyclist at peak effort: ~400 W. A light bulb: 60–100 W. A microwave: ~1000 W. These real-world comparisons help calibrate energy sense for AP problems asking if an answer is physically reasonable.

facts.correctAnswer: Kinetic, gravitational potential, elastic potential, and thermal energy in AP Physics 1
acceptableAlternatives: KE, gravitational PE, elastic PE, thermal energy
facts.quizQuestion: What are the four main forms of energy tracked in AP Physics 1 conservation problems?
facts.explanation: AP Physics 1 focuses on: (1) KE = ½mv² (kinetic energy of motion), (2) gravitational PE = mgh (stored in height), (3) elastic PE = ½kx² (stored in springs), and (4) thermal energy = fk·d (generated by friction). Conservation of energy: KE_i + PE_i = KE_f + PE_f + ΔE_thermal. Other forms (chemical, electrical, nuclear, sound) exist but are not quantitatively analyzed in this course.

facts.correctAnswer: W_friction = −f_k × d = −μk × N × d
acceptableAlternatives: -f_k * d

facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for work done by kinetic friction on an object sliding distance d?
facts.explanation: Kinetic friction force fk = μk·N acts opposite to motion (θ = 180°), so W_friction = fk·d·cos(180°) = −fk·d = −μk·N·d. This is always negative (removes energy from the object). The energy lost equals the thermal energy generated in the surfaces: ΔE_thermal = +μk·N·d. In a full energy equation: KE_f = KE_i + W_net = KE_i − μk·N·d for horizontal sliding.

facts.correctAnswer: Gravitational PE = mgh; h is measured from any chosen reference level
acceptableAlternatives: PE = mgh measured from a reference point you choose; only changes in PE matter
facts.quizQuestion: Why can you choose any reference level for gravitational potential energy?
facts.explanation: Only changes in gravitational PE (ΔPE = mgΔh) matter in energy conservation — not absolute values. You can set h = 0 anywhere convenient: the floor, the ground, the table top. The physics (ΔKE, ΔPE) works out the same regardless. Common choice: set h = 0 at the lowest point of motion so PE is never negative. The reference level choice should be stated and kept consistent throughout a problem.

facts.explanation: Work = ∫F dx, which geometrically equals the area under the F-vs-x curve. For a constant force (horizontal line), area = F × d = work. For a spring (F = kx, linear), area = ½ × base × height = ½kx² = elastic PE. For any variable force, calculate the total shaded area. Areas below the x-axis (negative force) represent negative work. This is one of the most common graph questions on AP Physics 1.

facts.quizQuestion: Does holding a heavy suitcase stationary require work (in the physics sense)?
facts.explanation: No. Work = F·d·cosθ requires displacement d > 0. Holding a stationary suitcase means d = 0, so W = 0 even though you exert a large upward force and your muscles fatigue. Physiologically your muscles are doing internal work (contracting and relaxing), but the physics definition of work is F·d. This distinction between biological and physics 'work' is frequently tested on AP exams.

facts.explanation: No — gravity is conservative, so its work depends only on vertical displacement h, not the path. Rolling down a ramp of length L at angle θ (with h = L sinθ) gives the same work as falling straight down h: W_gravity = mgh in both cases. This is why mgh is a valid potential energy function — it equals the work gravity would do to bring the object from that height to the reference level, regardless of route.

facts.quizQuestion: Using energy conservation, what maximum height does an object reach if launched straight up at speed v?
facts.explanation: At launch: KE = ½mv², PE = 0. At max height: KE = 0, PE = mgh. Conservation of energy: ½mv² = mgh → h = v²/(2g). For v = 10 m/s: h = 100/(19.6) ≈ 5.1 m. Note this is the same result as using v² = v₀² + 2aΔx (kinematics) with v = 0, a = −g. Energy methods and kinematics give identical results when there's no friction.

facts.explanation: Total mechanical energy E_mech = KE + PE (all forms: gravitational + elastic). It is conserved (stays constant throughout motion) ONLY when no non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance, applied forces) do work. When friction is present: E_mech,f = E_mech,i − ΔE_thermal. Energy is always conserved in the broadest sense (including thermal), but mechanical energy alone decreases in the presence of friction.

facts.explanation: Momentum p = mv is a vector quantity pointing in the same direction as velocity. In 1D, momentum can be positive (moving right) or negative (moving left). In collisions: if two equal-mass objects collide head-on and stick together, the total momentum (p₁ + p₂ = mv + m(−v) = 0) is zero — they stop! Ignoring direction leads to incorrect results. facts.quizQuestion: Why do airbags, crumple zones, and padded surfaces reduce injury in a crash?
facts.explanation: In a crash, the car must come from high speed to zero — a fixed change in momentum (impulse J = Δp). By Impulse = F·Δt, if Δt increases (longer crash duration), the average force F decreases proportionally. Airbags and crumple zones extend the collision time from milliseconds to tens of milliseconds, reducing peak force dramatically. The total impulse (change in momentum) is the same — only the time distribution of force changes.

facts.explanation: Impulse J = ∫F dt equals the area under the F-t curve. For a constant force, area = F × Δt. For a variable force (like during a collision), the area under the spike gives total impulse. By the impulse-momentum theorem, this area equals Δp = mv_f − mv_i. facts.explanation: If the object starts at rest (p_initial = 0), then p_final must also equal 0. The two pieces must have equal and opposite momenta: p₁ = −p₂, meaning m₁v₁ = −m₂v₂. facts.explanation: Conservation of momentum: initial total p = 0. After firing: p_bullet (forward) + p_gun (backward) = 0. So m_gun × v_gun = m_bullet × v_bullet in magnitude but opposite direction. The gun has much larger mass, so its recoil velocity is much smaller: v_gun = (m_bullet/m_gun) × v_bullet. facts.explanation: The CM velocity v_cm = (m₁v₁ + m₂v₂)/(m₁ + m₂) = total momentum / total mass. Since total momentum is conserved (no external forces), v_cm stays constant before, during, and after the collision — for both elastic and inelastic collisions. facts.quizQuestion: What is the more general form of Newton's 2nd Law that uses momentum instead of acceleration?
facts.explanation: F_net = Δp/Δt is the general form of Newton's 2nd Law. For constant mass, Δp/Δt = m(Δv/Δt) = ma, recovering the familiar form. But for variable mass systems (rockets expelling gas), you must use the momentum form. This formulation also immediately shows why large forces (like during collisions) occur when Δp is large and Δt is small: F = Δp/Δt grows as Δt shrinks.

facts.explanation: In an elastic collision: BOTH total kinetic energy AND total momentum are conserved. Billiard ball collisions approximate this. In an inelastic collision: momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is NOT (some converts to heat, sound, deformation). A perfectly inelastic collision (objects stick together) maximizes KE loss while still conserving momentum. ALL collisions conserve momentum; only elastic ones conserve KE.

facts.explanation: In a perfectly inelastic collision, objects stick together and move as one — this configuration minimizes post-collision KE. The KE lost goes to deformation, heat, and sound. facts.explanation: Follow-through extends the time the bat stays in contact with the ball, increasing Δt. By J = F·Δt = Δp, a longer contact time (even with the same average force) gives greater impulse = greater change in momentum = higher exit speed. The same principle applies in golf, tennis, and soccer. Stopping the swing abruptly (short Δt) reduces the impulse the bat imparts to the ball.

facts.explanation: Conservation of momentum follows directly from Newton's 2nd law: F_net,ext = Δp_total/Δt. If F_net,ext = 0 → Δp_total = 0 → total momentum is constant. facts.quizQuestion: What is the impulse-momentum theorem equation?
facts.explanation: The impulse-momentum theorem states J = Δp: the impulse delivered to an object equals its change in momentum. Since J = F·Δt and Δp = m·Δv (for constant mass): F·Δt = m(v_f − v_i). Both sides have units of kg·m/s (= N·s). This theorem is the foundation for analyzing collisions and the effect of force over time. It directly links kinematics (velocity change) with dynamics (force × time).

compute KE before and after using momentum conservation to find after
facts.quizQuestion: In a perfectly inelastic collision, what is the easiest method to find kinetic energy lost?
facts.explanation: Easiest method: (1) Use momentum conservation to find v_f = (m₁v₁ + m₂v₂)/(m₁+m₂). (2) Compute KE_i = ½m₁v₁² + ½m₂v₂². (3) Compute KE_f = ½(m₁+m₂)v_f². (4) ΔKE_lost = KE_i − KE_f. Always positive. The fraction lost is m₂/(m₁+m₂) when a mass m₁ hits stationary m₂. When m₂ >> m₁ (hitting a wall), virtually all KE is lost. AP exams typically ask students to compute this step by step.

facts.explanation: For equal masses in elastic collision: applying both momentum conservation (mv₁ = mv₁' + mv₂') and energy conservation (½mv₁² = ½mv₁'² + ½mv₂') simultaneously gives v₁' = 0 and v₂' = v₁. facts.explanation: The impulse approximation: during a very brief collision (Δt ~ milliseconds), the impulse from external forces (F_ext × Δt) is tiny compared to the impulse from the massive internal collision forces. For a car crash lasting 0.1 s, the collision force might be 10,000 N while gravity is only 800 N — gravity's contribution to impulse is 1% of the collision impulse. facts.quizQuestion: What unit is used for angular position and angular displacement in rotational kinematics?
facts.explanation: Angular displacement is measured in radians (rad). One full rotation = 2π ≈ 6.28 rad. 180° = π rad. 90° = π/2 rad. The radian is defined as the arc length divided by radius: θ = s/r. Because this is a ratio of two lengths, radians are technically dimensionless, but we write 'rad' as a label. Angular velocity (rad/s) and angular acceleration (rad/s²) use radians throughout.

The three equations become: ω = ω₀ + αt; θ = ω₀t + ½αt²; ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ. All the same strategies apply — choose the equation with the unknown and given quantities, check units (rad, rad/s, rad/s²).

facts.quizQuestion: Why does a uniform rod have a different moment of inertia when rotating about its center vs. about one end?
facts.explanation: I = Σmr² — mass farther from the axis contributes more (quadratically). A uniform rod rotating about its center: I = (1/12)ML². About one end: I = (1/3)ML² — four times larger! The mass near the center contributes little when rotating about the center, but all mass is relatively far when rotating about the end. This is why doors (hinged at one end) are harder to accelerate than spinning tops (mass near center).

facts.explanation: The lever arm (moment arm) is the perpendicular distance from the axis (pivot) to the line along which the force acts. Torque = F × (lever arm). Only the perpendicular component of force creates rotation — the parallel component just pulls/pushes toward/away from the pivot (zero torque). facts.explanation: Static equilibrium requires: (1) ΣF = 0 in all directions (no translational acceleration) AND (2) Στ = 0 about any chosen pivot (no rotational acceleration). facts.explanation: Torque is τ = r × F where r is the position vector from the pivot to where force is applied, and F is the force vector. The magnitude is |τ| = rF sinθ (θ = angle between r and F). facts.explanation: Setting Στ = 0 about the pivot: m₁g·d₁ = m₂g·d₂ → m₁d₁ = m₂d₂. Gravity cancels. A 80 kg person at 1 m requires a 40 kg person at 2 m to balance. facts.explanation: v = rω connects the rotational and translational descriptions of circular motion. A point farther from the axis (larger r) moves faster in a straight-line sense even though it rotates at the same ω. This is why the rim of a wheel moves faster than a point near the hub. Similarly, at = rα relates tangential acceleration to angular acceleration. These are the 'bridge' equations between linear and rotational quantities.

facts.explanation: Στ = Iα is the rotational form of Newton's 2nd Law: net torque = moment of inertia × angular acceleration. Compare: F↔τ, m↔I, a↔α. Larger I means harder to angularly accelerate (rotational inertia). A larger net torque produces greater angular acceleration. This equation governs spinning tops, wheels, and any rotating system. Units: N·m = kg·m²·(rad/s²).

facts.correctAnswer: Solid disk (I = ½MR²) reaches bottom faster; less rotational inertia than hollow ring (I = MR²)

facts.explanation: Both start with the same gravitational PE = mgh. At the bottom: E = ½mv² + ½Iω². With v = Rω: E = ½mv² + ½(I/R²)v². Disk: total KE factor = ½m + ½(½m) = ¾m → v_disk = √(4gh/3). Ring: factor = ½m + ½m = m → v_ring = √(gh). Disk wins because less energy goes to rotational KE, leaving more for translational KE. Shape wins: solid objects roll faster than hollow ones of the same shape.

A force applied at the chosen pivot has r = 0 and produces zero torque — it vanishes from the equation. facts.explanation: Rolling without slipping involves both translational motion (½mv²) and rotation (½Iω²). Using v = Rω: total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω² = ½mv² + ½(I/R²)v² = ½mv²(1 + I/mR²). facts.explanation: With no external torque, angular momentum L = Iω is conserved. When the skater pulls arms in, their moment of inertia I decreases (mass moved closer to rotation axis). Since L = Iω must stay constant: smaller I means larger ω — they spin faster. When arms go out, I increases, ω decreases. This is a spectacular demonstration of angular momentum conservation with no friction or external torque on the ice.

facts.explanation: Angular momentum conservation: if Στ_ext = 0 (no net external torque), then ΔL/Δt = 0 → L = constant. This is the rotational analog of linear momentum conservation (F_ext = 0 → p = constant). facts.explanation: Just as linear impulse J = F·Δt = Δp, angular impulse = τ·Δt = ΔL. A torque applied for a time Δt changes the angular momentum by ΔL = Iω_f − Iω_i. The area under a torque-vs-time graph equals ΔL. This applies to spinning up a flywheel (constant torque → L grows linearly) or applying braking torque to stop a rotating wheel.

facts.correctAnswer: Orbital speed v = √(GM/r); faster for lower orbits (closer to planet)

facts.explanation: Setting gravitational force equal to centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r → v = √(GM/r). Orbital speed DECREASES with larger radius — satellites in low Earth orbit (400 km) travel at ~7.8 km/s while geostationary satellites (35,786 km) travel at ~3.1 km/s. The Moon orbits at only ~1 km/s. This inverse-sqrt relationship comes directly from the inverse-square law of gravity.

acceptableAlternatives: geostationary orbit: T = 24 hr, satellite appears fixed over one location

facts.explanation: A geosynchronous orbit has period T = 24 hours (same as Earth's rotation), placing the satellite at ~35,786 km altitude. A satellite in this orbit above the equator appears stationary from the ground — a dish antenna can point at it without tracking. GPS satellites are in medium Earth orbit (~20,200 km, T ≈ 12 hours). The ISS is in low Earth orbit (T ≈ 90 min) and moves visibly across the sky.

facts.explanation: No external torque acts during the collapse, so L = Iω = constant. For a sphere, I ∝ MR². facts.explanation: Rotational work W = τ·Δθ is the analog of linear work W = F·d. The angle Δθ must be in radians. This allows energy methods in rotational problems: a motor applying 50 N·m torque through 10 rad does 500 J of work.

facts.correctAnswer: Solid sphere rolls faster than hollow sphere; smaller I means more translational KE

facts.quizQuestion: What happens to the amplitude of oscillation when a spring-mass system experiences friction?
facts.explanation: With friction, each oscillation loses energy (converted to thermal energy), so amplitude decreases exponentially over time — this is damped oscillation. Lightly damped: many oscillations before stopping. Critically damped: returns to equilibrium as fast as possible without oscillating (ideal for door closers). Overdamped: returns very slowly without oscillating. The period remains approximately the same while amplitude decreases.

Damping only affects frequency, not amplitude
Amplitude decreases but then rebounds to its original value

facts.correctAnswer: Driving force at the system natural frequency causes maximum amplitude buildup
acceptableAlternatives: resonance: f_drive = f_natural → maximum amplitude buildup
facts.quizQuestion: What is resonance in oscillatory systems, and why can it be destructive?
facts.explanation: Resonance occurs when an external driving force is applied at a frequency matching the system's natural frequency (f_0 = (1/2π)√(k/m)). Energy input at the natural frequency causes amplitude to grow dramatically. Real examples: soldiers break step on bridges (Angers bridge collapse, 1850), Tacoma Narrows Bridge (wind at resonance frequency), opera singers shattering glass. Damping limits the maximum amplitude at resonance — without damping, amplitude grows without bound.

facts.correctAnswer: Increasing mass increases period; increasing spring constant decreases period (T = 2π√(m/k))
acceptableAlternatives: heavier mass → longer period; stiffer spring → shorter period
facts.quizQuestion: How does doubling the mass or doubling the spring constant each affect the period of a spring-mass oscillator?
facts.explanation: T = 2π√(m/k). Doubling mass m: T_new = 2π√(2m/k) = √2 × T_original ≈ 1.41T (period increases by factor √2). Doubling spring constant k: T_new = 2π√(m/2k) = T/√2 ≈ 0.71T (period decreases by factor √2). Quadrupling mass gives double the period. These factor-of-√2 changes from doubling m or k are frequently tested on AP exams.

facts.correctAnswer: Increasing length increases period (T ∝ √L); increasing g decreases period (T ∝ 1/√g)
acceptableAlternatives: longer pendulum → longer T; stronger gravity → shorter T
facts.quizQuestion: A grandfather clock runs slow on the Moon (g_Moon = g_Earth/6). How should you adjust the pendulum to make it keep correct Moon time?
facts.explanation: T = 2π√(L/g). On the Moon: T_Moon = 2π√(L/g_Moon) = 2π√(6L/g_Earth) = √6 × T_Earth ≈ 2.45 × T_Earth. To restore correct period, shorten L by factor 6: new T = 2π√(L/6 / g_Moon) = 2π√(L/6 / (g_Earth/6)) = 2π√(L/g_Earth) = T_Earth. On Earth a grandfather clock's pendulum is typically about 1 m long; on the Moon it would need to be about 1/6 m long.

facts.correctAnswer: 1000 kg/m³ (or equivalently 1 g/cm³)
acceptableAlternatives: 1 g/mL
facts.quizQuestion: What is the density of fresh water, a key reference value in AP Physics 1 fluid problems?
facts.explanation: The density of fresh water is exactly 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³ at 4°C. Sea water is about 1025 kg/m³. Ice is about 917 kg/m³ (less than water — why ice floats). Objects less dense than 1000 kg/m³ float in water; denser objects sink. This is the reference density for buoyancy calculations. Air at sea level: ~1.2 kg/m³; iron: ~7874 kg/m³; lead: ~11,340 kg/m³.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the standard atmospheric pressure at sea level in SI units?
facts.explanation: Standard atmospheric pressure = 101,325 Pa ≈ 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa ≈ 101.3 kPa. This is often rounded to 10⁵ Pa for calculations. Gauge pressure measures pressure above (or below) atmospheric pressure. Absolute pressure = gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure. At sea level breathing air, every square centimeter of your body experiences about 10 N of atmospheric pressure (balanced by internal body pressure).

facts.correctAnswer: Absolute pressure = gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure; gauge is measured relative to atmosphere
acceptableAlternatives: P_abs = P_gauge + P_atm
facts.quizQuestion: A tire gauge reads 30 PSI (gauge). What is the actual (absolute) pressure inside the tire?
facts.explanation: Gauge pressure is measured above atmospheric pressure. Absolute pressure = gauge + atmospheric. At sea level, P_atm ≈ 14.7 PSI ≈ 101.3 kPa. So absolute tire pressure ≈ 30 + 14.7 ≈ 44.7 PSI ≈ 308 kPa. A flat tire reads 0 gauge pressure but 14.7 PSI absolute (still has atmospheric pressure inside). Vacuum gauges read negative (below atmospheric). Hydrostatic pressure calculations use absolute pressure.

facts.correctAnswer: Pressure at a given depth depends only on depth and fluid density, not the shape of the container
acceptableAlternatives: P = ρgh; independent of container shape or volume
facts.quizQuestion: A narrow cylinder and a wide tank are both filled with water to the same depth. Which has greater pressure at the bottom?
facts.explanation: Both have the same pressure at the bottom! P = ρgh depends only on fluid density ρ, g, and depth h — not on the container's shape, cross-sectional area, or total volume of water. This is the hydrostatic paradox: a narrow 10 m tube and a vast ocean both have the same pressure at 10 m depth (assuming same fluid density). The amount of fluid above does not matter — only the height (depth) does.

facts.correctAnswer: Pressure on enclosed fluid transmits equally throughout; enables hydraulic force multiplication
acceptableAlternatives: hydraulic press: F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂; larger piston exerts larger force
facts.quizQuestion: How does Pascal's Principle allow a small force to lift a heavy car in a hydraulic lift?
facts.explanation: Pascal's Principle: pressure added to any part of an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally to all parts. Pressure P = F/A is the same throughout. A small piston with area A₁ exerts pressure P = F₁/A₁. The large piston (area A₂) feels the same pressure: F₂ = P × A₂ = F₁(A₂/A₁). If A₂ = 100A₁, the output force is 100× the input — a force multiplier. But the small piston must move 100× farther (energy conservation: work in = work out).

facts.correctAnswer: Objects float if their average density < fluid density; sink if average density > fluid density
acceptableAlternatives: float when ρ_object < ρ_fluid; sink when ρ_object > ρ_fluid
facts.quizQuestion: What comparison of densities determines whether an object floats or sinks?
facts.explanation: An object floats if its average density is less than the fluid's density (buoyant force ≥ weight). Sinks if denser. Neutral buoyancy (suspended in fluid) at equal densities. Key: it's the AVERAGE density — a hollow steel ship floats because its average density (including air inside) is less than water, even though steel itself (7874 kg/m³) is much denser than water. Ice (917 kg/m³) floats in water (1000 kg/m³) with about 8% above surface.
distractors: Objects float based on their mass, not density

facts.correctAnswer: F_b = ρ_fluid × g × V_submerged (weight of fluid displaced)
acceptableAlternatives: buoyant force = density of fluid × g × submerged volume
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for the buoyant force on an object submerged in a fluid?
facts.explanation: Archimedes' Principle: F_buoyant = ρ_fluid × g × V_displaced. For a fully submerged object: V_displaced = volume of object. For a floating object: V_displaced = only the submerged portion. The buoyant force equals the weight of fluid displaced (not the weight of the object). F_b = ρ_f·g·V is independent of the object's density, material, or shape — only the displaced fluid volume matters.
distractors: F_b = ρ_fluid × g × V_total (regardless of submersion)

facts.correctAnswer: Where a pipe narrows, flow speed increases (A₁v₁ = A₂v₂); narrower = faster
acceptableAlternatives: smaller cross-section = higher speed; A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ = flow rate Q
facts.quizQuestion: Water flows through a pipe that narrows to half its original diameter. By what factor does the flow speed change?
facts.explanation: The continuity equation A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (flow rate conservation). Area depends on radius²: A = πr². Half diameter means quarter area (A₂ = A₁/4). So v₂ = v₁ × (A₁/A₂) = v₁ × 4 = 4v₁. Speed quadruples! This is why water from a garden hose shoots far when you cover part of the nozzle — smaller area forces higher speed. Blood speeds up through narrowed arteries (stenosis) by the same principle.

facts.correctAnswer: Higher speed flow has lower pressure (Bernoulli): airplane wings, Venturi tubes, curved ball spin
acceptableAlternatives: faster fluid → lower pressure; explains lift, Venturi effect, curveballs
facts.quizQuestion: What are three real-world applications of the Bernoulli effect (faster flow = lower pressure)?
facts.explanation: Bernoulli's principle (energy conservation in fluids): faster flow → lower pressure. Applications: (1) Airplane lift: air flows faster over curved upper wing surface → lower pressure above wing → net upward lift. (2) Venturi tube: fluid speeds up in narrow section → pressure drops → used in carburetors and flow meters. (3) Spinning baseball curves because spin creates faster airflow on one side → pressure difference → ball curves toward the low-pressure side.

facts.correctAnswer: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant along a streamline
acceptableAlternatives: P₁ + ½ρv₁² + ρgh₁ = P₂ + ½ρv₂² + ρgh₂
facts.quizQuestion: What is the full Bernoulli equation, and what does each term represent?
facts.explanation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant. This is energy conservation per unit volume along a streamline: P (pressure energy density), ½ρv² (kinetic energy density), ρgh (gravitational PE density). If height is constant (horizontal pipe): P + ½ρv² = constant, so higher v → lower P. If velocity is negligible (static fluid): P + ρgh = constant → hydrostatic pressure. Bernoulli assumes incompressible, non-viscous, steady flow along a streamline.

facts.correctAnswer: Viscosity is resistance to flow; laminar = smooth layers; turbulent = chaotic eddies
acceptableAlternatives: viscosity = fluid friction; laminar = orderly layers; turbulent = chaotic mixing
facts.quizQuestion: What is viscosity, and what distinguishes laminar from turbulent flow?
facts.explanation: Viscosity measures a fluid's internal resistance to flow — it's analogous to friction. Honey has high viscosity; water has low viscosity. Laminar flow: fluid moves in smooth parallel layers with no mixing between layers — occurs at low speeds or in small tubes. Turbulent flow: chaotic, with eddies and cross-flow mixing — occurs at high speeds or large pipe diameters. AP Physics 1 treats viscosity qualitatively only; Bernoulli's equation assumes ideal (non-viscous) fluid.

Viscosity is the pressure difference across a fluid element

facts.correctAnswer: Barometer measures atmospheric pressure; manometer measures gauge pressure of a gas
acceptableAlternatives: barometer = atmospheric pressure device; manometer = gas pressure measurement device
facts.quizQuestion: What is the difference between a barometer and a manometer?
facts.explanation: A barometer measures atmospheric pressure using a closed mercury column (P_atm = ρ_Hg × g × h ≈ 760 mmHg = 101.3 kPa at sea level). A manometer measures the gauge pressure of a gas by comparing it to atmospheric or vacuum reference. Open manometer: P_gas − P_atm = ρ × g × Δh. Both work by balancing fluid weight (ρgh) against the pressure being measured. Mercury is used because its high density (13,600 kg/m³) means practical column heights (0.76 m vs 10 m for water).

facts.correctAnswer: A floating object displaces fluid equal to its weight: F_b = W_object (equilibrium)
acceptableAlternatives: floating: buoyant force = weight; ρ_fluid × g × V_sub = ρ_obj × g × V_total
facts.quizQuestion: What fraction of an ice cube floats above water given that ice density is 917 kg/m³ and water density is 1000 kg/m³?
facts.explanation: For a floating object: F_b = W → ρ_water × g × V_sub = ρ_ice × g × V_total → V_sub/V_total = ρ_ice/ρ_water = 917/1000 = 0.917. So 91.7% is submerged and 8.3% is above water. This is why 'tip of the iceberg' (about 10% visible) is an apt metaphor for hidden dangers. Sea ice floats with about 8% above; icebergs (fresh ice, ρ ≈ 900 kg/m³) float with about 10% above.

facts.correctAnswer: P = P₀ + ρgh (absolute pressure at depth h = surface pressure plus ρgh)
acceptableAlternatives: P = P_surface + rho*g*h
facts.quizQuestion: What is the pressure at depth h in a fluid of density ρ with surface pressure P₀?
facts.explanation: P = P₀ + ρgh where P₀ is pressure at the surface (usually atmospheric ≈ 101,325 Pa), ρ is fluid density, g = 9.8 m/s², h is depth below surface. At 10 m depth in water: P = 101,325 + (1000)(9.8)(10) = 101,325 + 98,000 ≈ 200,000 Pa ≈ 2 atm. Pressure approximately doubles for every 10 m of depth. This is why scuba divers must equalize their ears — pressure changes rapidly with depth.
distractors: P = P₀ − ρgh (pressure decreases with depth)

facts.correctAnswer: Neutral buoyancy: object's average density exactly equals fluid density; buoyant force equals weight
acceptableAlternatives: neutrally buoyant: ρ_object = ρ_fluid; F_b = mg; object suspended at any depth
facts.quizQuestion: What is neutral buoyancy and how do submarines achieve it?
facts.explanation: Neutral buoyancy means an object is suspended at a given depth — it neither rises nor sinks. This requires the buoyant force to exactly equal the gravitational force, which occurs when ρ_object = ρ_fluid. Submarines achieve neutral buoyancy by adjusting ballast tanks: pumping water IN to increase average density (to sink) or pumping water OUT with compressed air to decrease average density (to rise). At neutral buoyancy, the submarine hovers without using engine power.

facts.correctAnswer: Flow rate Q = Av has units of m³/s (volume per unit time)
acceptableAlternatives: Q = A × v; volumetric flow rate in cubic meters per second
facts.quizQuestion: What does the continuity equation A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ = Q represent, and what are the units of Q?
facts.explanation: Q = Av is the volumetric flow rate — the volume of fluid passing a cross-section per unit time. Units: m² × m/s = m³/s. An incompressible fluid in a pipe must have the same flow rate at every cross-section (no fluid is created or destroyed). This is conservation of mass expressed for fluids. A pipe of area 0.01 m² with fluid flowing at 2 m/s has Q = 0.02 m³/s = 20 liters/s.
